name: 〜か
description:
か turns a statement into a yes/no question when placed at the end of a sentence: これは本ですか (Is this a book?). In polite speech, か is the standard question marker. It can also embed questions inside larger sentences as an indirect question marker.

In casual speech, か can sound blunt or even challenging, especially with a falling intonation, so it is often dropped and replaced by a rising intonation in informal conversation. か also appears in expressions of uncertainty: 行くかどうか分からない (I don't know whether I'll go).

examples.japanese: 日本語を話しますか？
examples.romaji: Nihongo o hanashimasu ka?
examples.english: Do you speak Japanese?

examples.japanese: これはあなたのかばんですか？
examples.romaji: Kore wa anata no kaban desu ka?
examples.english: Is this your bag?

notes: In casual Japanese, questions are often formed without か — just a rising intonation on the final word. Using か with a falling tone can sound confrontational between friends. か+な (かな) softens the question into a wondering-aloud expression: 明日は晴れるかな (I wonder if it'll be sunny tomorrow).

name: 〜が
description:
The subject marker が identifies the grammatical subject of a sentence, emphasizing who or what performs the action or possesses the quality. It introduces new, unknown, or specifically identified information — answering implied questions like 'who did it?' or 'which one?'.

が is required after interrogative words (誰が、何が) and in relative clauses where the subject of the embedded clause is marked. It also expresses potential, desire, and ability when used with verbs like できる, わかる, 好き, 嫌い, and ほしい.

examples.japanese: 猫が庭にいます。
examples.romaji: Neko ga niwa ni imasu.
examples.english: There is a cat in the garden.

examples.japanese: 雨が降っています。
examples.romaji: Ame ga futte imasu.
examples.english: It is raining.

notes: は vs が is one of the hardest distinctions in Japanese. Use が when the subject is new information or being contrasted/emphasized; use は when the topic is already established in context. In subordinate clauses, が is far more common than は.

name: 〜か〜か
description:
か〜か is used to present alternatives or choices, meaning "A or B", "whether A or B". It connects two options (nouns, verb phrases, or adjective phrases) and is used in questions, indirect questions, and expressions of uncertainty.

When used in embedded questions (indirect questions), the entire か〜か clause acts as a noun phrase. In direct questions it simply lists options for the listener to choose.
usage: Noun + か + Noun + か / Verb (plain) + か + Verb (plain) + か

examples.japanese: コーヒーか紅茶か、どちらにしますか。
examples.romaji: Koohii ka koucha ka, dochira ni shimasu ka.
examples.english: Coffee or tea — which will you have?

examples.japanese: 行くか行かないか、まだ決めていません。
examples.romaji: Iku ka ikanai ka, mada kimete imasen.
examples.english: I haven't decided yet whether to go or not.

notes: The pattern か〜か in embedded questions (indirect questions) is distinct from its use in direct questions. In indirect questions it forms a noun clause: 行くかどうか分かりません (I don't know whether I'll go). かどうか is a simplified form of か〜か that is used with a single option.

name: 〜から
description:
から has two main uses. As a particle after nouns, it means 'from' — marking the starting point of time or space: 東京から大阪まで (from Tokyo to Osaka), 9時から (from 9 o'clock). As a conjunction after verb/adjective plain forms, it means 'because' — stating the reason for a following action or state: 雨が降っているから、外に出ない (because it's raining, I won't go out).

The causal から comes after the reason clause and before the result clause. It is more direct and assertive than ので, which is softer and more polite. から is preferred in casual speech; ので is preferred in formal or written contexts.
usage: Noun + から / Clause + から

examples.japanese: 九時から授業が始まります。
examples.romaji: Ku-ji kara jugyō ga hajimarimasu.
examples.english: Class starts from nine o'clock.

examples.japanese: 眠いから、もう寝ます。
examples.romaji: Nemui kara, mō nemasu.
examples.english: Because I'm sleepy, I'll go to sleep now.

notes: から (because) is more subjective and assertive — the speaker confidently states their reason. ので (because) is more objective and indirect — better for formal apologies, requests, and writing. Using から too much in formal speech can sound blunt.

name: 〜が見える
description:
見える (みえる) means "to be visible" or "to be able to see" — it describes something coming into view or being perceptible without deliberate effort. The thing that is seen is marked with が, not を. Unlike 見る (to look at/watch, deliberate action), 見える is a potential/stative verb.

A secondary meaning of 見える is "to appear" or "to seem" — giving a subjective impression: 彼は若く見える (He looks young). This usage expresses appearance, not necessarily reality.
usage: Noun + が + 見える / Noun + に + 見える (appears as)

examples.japanese: ここから山が見えます。
examples.romaji: Koko kara yama ga miemasu.
examples.english: You can see the mountains from here.

examples.japanese: 彼女は年より若く見える。
examples.romaji: Kanojo wa toshi yori wakaku mieru.
examples.english: She looks younger than her age.

notes: 見える (mieru) = can be seen/looks like (spontaneous perception) vs 見る (miru) = to look/watch (deliberate). Similarly: 聞こえる = can be heard vs 聞く = to listen. These pairs are important at N5.

name: 〜が聞こえる
description:
聞こえる (きこえる) means "to be audible" or "to be able to hear" — it describes sound reaching the listener without deliberate effort. The sound is marked with が. It is the spontaneous counterpart to 聞く (to listen/ask, deliberate action).

Like 見える, 聞こえる can also mean "to sound like" or "to come across as": 変に聞こえるかもしれませんが (This might sound strange, but…). This usage describes how something is perceived acoustically.

examples.japanese: 外から子どもたちの声が聞こえる。
examples.romaji: Soto kara kodomo-tachi no koe ga kikoeru.
examples.english: I can hear the children's voices from outside.

examples.japanese: 遠くで音楽が聞こえました。
examples.romaji: Tooku de ongaku ga kikoemashita.
examples.english: I could hear music in the distance.

notes: 聞こえる (kikoeru) = can be heard/sounds like (spontaneous) vs 聞く (kiku) = to listen/hear deliberately or to ask. Compare the pair: 見える/見る :: 聞こえる/聞く. Both 見える and 聞こえる take が for the perceived object.

name: 〜が欲しい
description:
欲しい is an i-adjective meaning "to want (something)". The desired item is marked with が. It expresses the speaker's own desire for a noun — not actions (for wanting to do things, use 〜たい).

Because 欲しい describes a personal feeling, using it to describe what a third person wants is considered presumptuous. To express what someone else wants, use 欲しがっている (is showing signs of wanting) instead.
usage: Noun + が + 欲しい / Noun + が + 欲しかった (past)

examples.japanese: 新しいコンピューターが欲しい。
examples.romaji: Atarashii konpyuutaa ga hoshii.
examples.english: I want a new computer.

examples.japanese: 子どもの頃、犬が欲しかった。
examples.romaji: Kodomo no koro, inu ga hoshikatta.
examples.english: When I was a child, I wanted a dog.

notes: 欲しい vs 〜たい: 欲しい is used with nouns (I want a thing); 〜たい is used with verbs (I want to do something). Third-person: 彼は犬を欲しがっている (He seems to want a dog).

name: 〜けど・けれども
description:
けど (casual) and けれども (formal) are conjunctions meaning "but", "however", or "although". They connect two clauses where the second contrasts with or qualifies the first. けれど is a mid-register form. All three can appear mid-sentence or at the end of a sentence.

When used at the end of a sentence (ちょっと難しいんですけど…), they create a softened, expectant tone — implying an unstated consequence or request. This is very common in polite, indirect Japanese communication.

examples.japanese: 行きたいけど、時間がない。
examples.romaji: Ikitai kedo, jikan ga nai.
examples.english: I want to go, but I don't have time.

examples.japanese: 高いけれども、買います。
examples.romaji: Takai keredomo, kaimasu.
examples.english: It's expensive, but I'll buy it.

notes: Register: けど (casual) → けれど (neutral) → けれども (formal) → が (very formal/written). All mean "but". The trailing use (〜ですけど…) is a softening strategy in Japanese to avoid being too direct.

name: 〜ことができる
description:
〜ことができる expresses ability or possibility — 'can,' 'be able to.' The dictionary form of the verb is followed by ことができる. It is more formal than the potential form of the verb (e.g., 食べられる) and often preferred in writing and formal speech.

The negative is ことができない (cannot). The past is ことができた (was able to). This pattern works with all verb types uniformly, making it easier to use than memorizing individual potential verb conjugations.

examples.japanese: 私は日本語を話すことができます。
examples.romaji: Watashi wa Nihongo o hanasu koto ga dekimasu.
examples.english: I can speak Japanese.

examples.japanese: ここで写真を撮ることができますか？
examples.romaji: Koko de shashin o toru koto ga dekimasu ka?
examples.english: Can I take photos here?

notes: 〜ことができる and the potential form (〜られる / 〜える) are largely interchangeable, but ことができる is more formal. In conversation, the potential form is more natural: 泳げる is more casual than 泳ぐことができる. Note that できる alone (without こと) means 'to be completed' or 'to be made.'

name: 〜すぎる
description:
〜すぎる attaches to verb stems or adjective stems to mean 'too much' or 'excessively.' For verbs: 食べすぎる (eat too much). For い-adjectives: remove い and add すぎる — 高すぎる (too expensive). For な-adjectives: add すぎる — きれいすぎる (too pretty, excessively so).

すぎる conjugates like a regular る-verb: すぎた (was too much), すぎて (too much and...). The て-form is common in explaining problems: 食べすぎて気持ちが悪い (I feel sick from eating too much). The nuance is nearly always negative — something is beyond an acceptable degree.
usage: Verb stem / Adj stem + すぎる

examples.japanese: 昨日飲みすぎました。
examples.romaji: Kinō nomisugimashita.
examples.english: I drank too much yesterday.

examples.japanese: このカバンは高すぎる。
examples.romaji: Kono kaban wa takasugiru.
examples.english: This bag is too expensive.

notes: すぎる can sometimes be used humorously or as an exaggerated compliment (かっこよすぎる! — too cool!), but the literal meaning is always 'excessively.' Don't use すぎる when you mean 'very' (とても) — すぎる always implies a problematic excess.

name: 〜ずつ
description:
〜ずつ attaches to numbers or quantities and means "each," "at a time," or "little by little." It indicates an equal distribution or a repeated increment: 一つずつ (one at a time), 少しずつ (little by little).

ずつ also expresses gradual change over time: 日本語が少しずつ上手になってきた (my Japanese has been getting better little by little). The quantity before ずつ specifies the unit of repetition or distribution.

examples.japanese: 薬を一日三回、二錠ずつ飲んでください。
examples.romaji: Kusuri wo ichinichi sankai, nijou zutsu nonde kudasai.
examples.english: Please take two tablets of the medicine three times a day.

examples.japanese: 少しずつ日本語が話せるようになった。
examples.romaji: Sukoshi zutsu nihongo ga hanaseru you ni natta.
examples.english: I have gradually become able to speak Japanese.

examples.japanese: クラス全員に一枚ずつ紙を配ってください。
examples.romaji: Kurasu zenin ni ichimai zutsu kami wo kubatte kudasai.
examples.english: Please hand out one sheet of paper to each member of the class.

notes: Common combinations: 少しずつ (little by little), 一人ずつ (one person at a time), 一つずつ (one by one). ずつ always follows a quantity expression.

name: 〜たい
description:
〜たい expresses a first-person desire to do something — 'want to.' It attaches to the verb stem: 食べたい (want to eat), 行きたい (want to go). It conjugates as an い-adjective: 食べたくない (don't want to eat), 食べたかった (wanted to eat).

たい is generally only used for the speaker's own desires in declarative sentences. Asking about a second person's desires uses たい as a question: 何が食べたいですか (what do you want to eat?). For third-person desires, 〜たがっている is used instead: 彼は行きたがっている (he seems to want to go).

examples.japanese: 日本に行きたいです。
examples.romaji: Nihon ni ikitai desu.
examples.english: I want to go to Japan.

examples.japanese: ピザが食べたい。
examples.romaji: Piza ga tabetai.
examples.english: I want to eat pizza.

notes: Never use たい to describe a third person's internal desire in a plain declarative sentence — say 彼は行きたがっている, not 彼は行きたい. The object of たい can be marked with either が or を: ラーメンが/を食べたい (both are acceptable).

name: 〜たことがある
description:
〜たことがある expresses past experience — having done something at least once at some unspecified point in the past: 富士山に登ったことがある (I have climbed Mt. Fuji before). It is equivalent to the English present perfect used for life experience.

The pattern uses the た-form (past plain form) of the verb + ことがある. The negative — 〜たことがない — means 'have never done': 刺身を食べたことがない (I've never eaten sashimi). The emphasis is on whether or not the experience has occurred, not when.

examples.japanese: 富士山に登ったことがあります。
examples.romaji: Fujisan ni nobotta koto ga arimasu.
examples.english: I have climbed Mt. Fuji before.

examples.japanese: 納豆を食べたことがない。
examples.romaji: Nattō o tabeta koto ga nai.
examples.english: I have never eaten natto.

notes: 〜たことがある refers to life experience, not a specific recent past action. Compare: 昨日食べた (ate yesterday — simple past) vs 食べたことがある (have eaten at some point — experience). Don't use this pattern for things that just happened.

name: 〜たり〜たりする
description:
〜たり〜たりする lists actions in a non-exhaustive way, implying 'do things like A and B (among other things).' It is formed from the た-form of each verb + り, ending with する: 週末は読んだり映画を見たりします (on weekends I do things like reading and watching movies).

The pattern conveys that the listed actions are representative examples, not a complete list. It can also describe alternating or contrasting actions: 泣いたり笑ったりする (alternate between crying and laughing). Using only one 〜たり is possible in casual speech but sounds incomplete to many speakers.

examples.japanese: 週末は本を読んだり、映画を見たりします。
examples.romaji: Shūmatsu wa hon o yondari, eiga o mitari shimasu.
examples.english: On weekends I do things like read books and watch movies.

examples.japanese: 子供たちは走ったり、歌ったりしていた。
examples.romaji: Kodomo-tachi wa hashittari, utattari shite ita.
examples.english: The children were running around and singing, among other things.

notes: Always end the たり〜たり construction with する (or its conjugated form). Using たり without する is technically incomplete. The list is always non-exhaustive — if you want to list everything, use the て-form chain instead.

name: 〜だろう
description:
だろう expresses conjecture or supposition in plain/informal speech, meaning "probably", "I think", or "right?". It is the plain equivalent of the polite でしょう. When used with rising intonation at the end, it seeks confirmation from the listener.

だろう follows the plain form of verbs, i-adjectives, na-adjectives (drop だ → だろう), and nouns. It is common in written language, news, and male speech.
usage: Verb (plain) + だろう / い-adj + だろう / な-adj stem + だろう / Noun + だろう

examples.japanese: 明日は雨が降るだろう。
examples.romaji: Ashita wa ame ga furu darou.
examples.english: It will probably rain tomorrow.

examples.japanese: 彼はまだ知らないだろう。
examples.romaji: Kare wa mada shiranai darou.
examples.english: He probably doesn't know yet.

notes: In conversational speech, だろう often contracts to だろ. The feminine equivalent is でしょう used in all registers, while だろう is more commonly associated with male casual speech.

name: 〜で
description:
で marks the location where an action takes place (図書館で勉強する — study at the library), distinguishing it from に which marks existence. It also indicates the means or tool used to perform an action: 電車で行く (go by train), 英語で話す (speak in English).

Additionally, で can express cause or reason (病気で休んだ — took time off due to illness), scope or limit (日本一で終わった — ended with first place in Japan), and total quantity (三人で食べた — ate with three people).

examples.japanese: 図書館で勉強します。
examples.romaji: Toshokan de benkyō shimasu.
examples.english: I study at the library.

examples.japanese: バスで学校に行きます。
examples.romaji: Basu de gakkō ni ikimasu.
examples.english: I go to school by bus.

notes: The most common confusion is に vs で for location. Remember: に = where something exists or arrives (いる/ある/行く); で = where an action is performed (食べる/勉強する/働く).

name: 〜ている
description:
〜ている expresses either an ongoing action (progressive aspect) or a resultant state that persists after an action is completed. 食べている means 'is eating' (ongoing). 結婚している means 'is married' — the state of having gotten married persists (resultant state).

Whether ている expresses progressive or resultant state depends on the verb type: action verbs (食べる, 書く) → progressive; change-of-state verbs (結婚する, 死ぬ, 起きる) → resultant state. Some verbs (知る, 持つ) only naturally appear in resultant state: 知っている (knows), 持っている (has/holds).

examples.japanese: 今、音楽を聴いています。
examples.romaji: Ima, ongaku o kiite imasu.
examples.english: I am listening to music now.

examples.japanese: 兄は結婚しています。
examples.romaji: Ani wa kekkon shite imasu.
examples.english: My older brother is married.

notes: In casual speech, ている contracts to てる: 食べてる, 知ってる. The contraction is extremely common and natural in conversation. Using the full ている in casual contexts can sound overly formal.

name: 〜てください
description:
〜てください is a polite request form meaning "please do ~". It is formed by attaching ください to the て-form of a verb. This is the standard way to make polite requests in Japanese.

Standalone ください (without a verb) means "please give me ~" and follows a noun + を. The combination てください is among the most frequently used structures in everyday Japanese.
usage: Verb (て-form) + ください / Noun + をください

examples.japanese: もう一度言ってください。
examples.romaji: Mou ichido itte kudasai.
examples.english: Please say it one more time.

examples.japanese: メニューをください。
examples.romaji: Menyuu wo kudasai.
examples.english: Please give me the menu.

notes: To make a negative request ("please don't ~"), use 〜ないでください: 写真を撮らないでください (Please don't take photos). ください comes from くださる, the honorific form of くれる.

name: 〜でしょう
description:
でしょう is the polite form of だろう and expresses probability or conjecture: "probably", "I think", "right?". It is used in formal speech, news broadcasts, and weather forecasts to state what is likely.

When said with rising intonation, でしょう seeks confirmation or agreement from the listener ("…right?" / "don't you think?"). With falling intonation it simply states a probability.
usage: Verb (plain) + でしょう / い-adj + でしょう / な-adj stem + でしょう / Noun + でしょう

examples.japanese: 明日は晴れるでしょう。
examples.romaji: Ashita wa hareru deshou.
examples.english: It will probably be sunny tomorrow.

examples.japanese: この問題は難しいでしょう。
examples.romaji: Kono mondai wa muzukashii deshou.
examples.english: This problem is probably difficult, right?

notes: でしょう and だろう have the same meaning; でしょう is polite/neutral while だろう is plain/informal. Both can express mild surprise or emphasis when said with stress: そうでしょう! (I knew it!).

name: 〜です
description:
です is the polite copula meaning 'is/am/are.' It follows nouns and な-adjectives to form polite predicates: 学生です (I am a student), きれいです (it is pretty). It is the polite counterpart of the plain-form copula だ.

です conjugates for tense and negation: present (です), past (でした), negative (ではありません / じゃありません), negative past (ではありませんでした). After い-adjectives, です is purely a politeness marker — the adjective itself carries the tense information: 暑いです (it is hot), 暑かったです (it was hot).
usage: Noun / い-Adj / な-Adj + です

examples.japanese: 山田さんは先生です。
examples.romaji: Yamada-san wa sensei desu.
examples.english: Mr. Yamada is a teacher.

examples.japanese: この花はきれいです。
examples.romaji: Kono hana wa kirei desu.
examples.english: This flower is beautiful.

notes: です does not translate directly in all contexts — it often just marks politeness level. The plain form is だ, used in casual speech. Note that です after い-adjectives is grammatically a polite suffix, not a true copula; the adjective still conjugates: 高くないです (it is not expensive).

name: 〜てはいけない
description:
〜てはいけない expresses prohibition — 'must not,' 'you cannot,' 'it is forbidden.' The て-form of the verb is followed by はいけない. It is a strong prohibition, often used for rules, warnings, and general social norms: 走ってはいけません (you must not run).

Shorter forms exist: 〜てはだめ (casual), 〜てはならない (more formal/literary). In casual speech, 〜ちゃだめ and 〜ちゃいけない are common contractions. The opposite pattern, permission, is 〜てもいい.

examples.japanese: ここでタバコを吸ってはいけません。
examples.romaji: Koko de tabako o sutte wa ikemasen.
examples.english: You must not smoke here.

examples.japanese: 試験中に話してはいけない。
examples.romaji: Shiken-chū ni hanashite wa ikenai.
examples.english: You must not talk during the exam.

notes: 〜てはいけない is stronger and more absolute than 〜ないほうがいい (it's better not to). Use いけない for firm prohibitions (rules, safety); use ないほうがいい for softer advice or recommendations.

name: 〜てもいい
description:
〜てもいい expresses permission — 'it is okay to,' 'you may.' The て-form of the verb is followed by もいい (or もいいです for politeness). It grants or requests permission: ここに座ってもいいですか (may I sit here?)

The pattern can also be used to give someone permission proactively: もう帰ってもいいよ (you can go home now). The negative equivalent, 〜てはいけない, expresses prohibition. A more casual equivalent is 〜ていい.

examples.japanese: ここに座ってもいいですか？
examples.romaji: Koko ni suwatte mo ii desu ka?
examples.english: May I sit here?

examples.japanese: 窓を開けてもいいですよ。
examples.romaji: Mado o akete mo ii desu yo.
examples.english: You may open the window.

notes: 〜てもいいですか is the standard polite way to ask for permission. More casual: 〜ていい? Even more formal/indirect: 〜てもよろしいでしょうか. The も in the pattern has an implicit 'even if you do X, it's fine' nuance.

name: 〜と
description:
と has two core uses as a particle. First, it connects nouns in an exhaustive list — 猫と犬 (cats and dogs, referring to both specifically) — unlike や which implies the list is incomplete. Second, と marks a quotation or thought: 彼は来ると言った (he said he'd come); 面白いと思う (I think it's interesting).

と also appears as a conditional particle meaning 'when/if (always true)': 春になると、桜が咲く (when spring comes, the cherry blossoms bloom). This conditional use implies a natural or inevitable result.
usage: Noun + と + Noun / Clause + と + 言う

examples.japanese: パンとバターを買いました。
examples.romaji: Pan to batā o kaimashita.
examples.english: I bought bread and butter.

examples.japanese: 友達と映画を見ました。
examples.romaji: Tomodachi to eiga o mimashita.
examples.english: I watched a movie with a friend.

notes: Use と for exhaustive, complete lists (exactly these items); use や for partial, representative lists (things like these). The quotation と is essential for reporting speech and thoughts — it precedes 言う, 思う, 聞く, and similar verbs.

name: 〜ところだ
description:
〜ところだ expresses the timing of an action relative to speaking. With the dictionary form: "about to do" (今から出かけるところだ — I am just about to go out). With て+いる: "in the middle of doing" (ご飯を食べているところだ). With the た-form: "just finished" (今帰ったところだ — I just got home).

All three usages emphasize precise timing. This makes ところだ more specific than using the present or past tense alone.
usage: Verb (dict.) + ところだ / Verb (て) + いるところだ / Verb (た) + ところだ

examples.japanese: ちょうど今、電話しようとしていたところです。
examples.romaji: Choudo ima, denwa shiyou to shite ita tokoro desu.
examples.english: I was just about to call you.

examples.japanese: 今レポートを書いているところです。
examples.romaji: Ima repooto wo kaite iru tokoro desu.
examples.english: I am in the middle of writing a report right now.

examples.japanese: 彼女は今起きたところだ。
examples.romaji: Kanojo wa ima okita tokoro da.
examples.english: She just woke up.

notes: Compare with ところに (just when an event occurs) and ところを (in the act of doing).

name: 〜ない
description:
〜ない is the plain negative form of verbs, meaning 'do not' or 'does not.' For Group 1 (u-verbs): change the final vowel to 'a' and add ない (書く → 書かない). For Group 2 (ru-verbs): remove る and add ない (食べる → 食べない). Irregular: する → しない, くる → こない.

The negative of い-adjectives is formed by removing い and adding くない: 高い → 高くない. For な-adjectives and nouns with だ, the negative is ではない / じゃない. ない itself is an い-adjective and conjugates accordingly: なかった (past negative).
usage: Verb (nai-form) / い-Adj stem + くない

examples.japanese: お酒を飲まない。
examples.romaji: Osake o nomanai.
examples.english: I don't drink alcohol.

examples.japanese: この映画は面白くない。
examples.romaji: Kono eiga wa omoshirokunai.
examples.english: This movie is not interesting.

notes: A common mistake is forming u-verb negatives incorrectly. Verbs ending in -u (買う → 買わない, NOT 買あない) are irregular — the -u ending becomes -wa before ない. Always check for this pattern.

name: 〜ないで
description:
〜ないで has two primary uses. First, it means "without doing ~" — describing an action performed without a preceding action (朝ごはんを食べないで学校へ行った = went to school without eating breakfast). Second, when followed by ください, it makes a negative request: "please don't ~".

The negative te-form ないで connects clauses or ends a sentence. It is distinct from ずに (more formal/literary "without doing") and なくて (which focuses on state rather than action).
usage: Verb (ない-form) + で / Verb (ない-form) + でください

examples.japanese: 傘を持たないで出かけてしまった。
examples.romaji: Kasa wo motanaide dekakete shimatta.
examples.english: I went out without bringing an umbrella.

examples.japanese: ここで写真を撮らないでください。
examples.romaji: Koko de shashin wo toranaide kudasai.
examples.english: Please don't take photos here.

notes: ないで vs なくて: ないで is used to connect actions (without doing A, do B) or make negative requests. なくて connects states or gives reasons (because I don't have time, I can't go).

name: 〜なさい
description:
なさい is a command form that expresses a directive to do something. It is firmer than てください (polite request) but softer than the blunt plain imperative (食べろ). It is widely used by parents, teachers, and in instructional contexts.

It is formed by dropping ます from the polite form and adding なさい. The command is direct but not rude in appropriate contexts — it would sound strange if a subordinate used it to a superior.

examples.japanese: 早く宿題をしなさい。
examples.romaji: Hayaku shukudai wo shinasai.
examples.english: Do your homework quickly.

examples.japanese: もっと野菜を食べなさい。
examples.romaji: Motto yasai wo tabenasai.
examples.english: Eat more vegetables.

notes: In casual/quick speech, なさい sometimes shortens to な: 食べな (eat). This shortened form is even more informal and mainly heard in Western Japanese dialects or casual speech.

name: 〜など
description:
〜など means "things like," "such as," or "etc." It follows nouns to show the item is one of several examples: 野菜や果物などを買った (I bought vegetables, fruit, and things like that). Similar to 〜とか but slightly more formal.

など can also carry a self-deprecating nuance: 私などにはできません (someone like me could not do it). In lists, など comes after the last item to signal the list is incomplete.

examples.japanese: 趣味はサッカーや野球などのスポーツです。
examples.romaji: Shumi wa sakkaa ya yakyuu nado no supootsu desu.
examples.english: My hobbies are sports like soccer and baseball.

examples.japanese: 東京や大阪などの大都市に住んでいる。
examples.romaji: Toukyou ya Oosaka nado no daitoshi ni sunde iru.
examples.english: I live in a big city like Tokyo or Osaka.

examples.japanese: 私などがそんな賞をもらえるとは思いませんでした。
examples.romaji: Watashi nado ga sonna shou wo moraeru to wa omoimasen deshita.
examples.english: I never thought someone like me could receive such an award.

notes: なんか is the more casual spoken equivalent. などと言う adds a dismissive nuance about a quoted statement.

name: 〜に
description:
に is one of the most versatile particles in Japanese, covering several core meanings. As a direction marker, it indicates the destination of movement: 学校に行く (go to school). As a location marker for existence, it shows where something is or someone stays: 家に犬がいる (there's a dog at home). As a time marker, it pinpoints a specific time: 三時に会いましょう (let's meet at 3 o'clock).

に also marks the recipient of an action (友達に手紙を書く — write a letter to a friend), the purpose of movement (買い物に行く — go for shopping), and the agent in passive constructions (先生に褒められた — was praised by the teacher).

examples.japanese: 学校に行きます。
examples.romaji: Gakkō ni ikimasu.
examples.english: I go to school.

examples.japanese: 机の上に本があります。
examples.romaji: Tsukue no ue ni hon ga arimasu.
examples.english: There is a book on the desk.

notes: Distinguish に from で: に marks existence location (〜にいる/ある) and destination, while で marks the location of an action (〜で食べる). Also distinguish に from へ: both mark direction but に emphasizes arrival at a point, while へ emphasizes the direction of travel.

name: 〜ね
description:
ね is a sentence-final particle that seeks confirmation or shared agreement from the listener, similar to 'right?', 'isn't it?', or 'don't you think?' It presupposes that the listener shares the same information or feeling: いい天気ですね (Nice weather, isn't it?).

ね softens statements and makes conversation feel more collaborative and warm. It is extremely common in everyday Japanese. The longer ねえ can express mild surprise or draw the listener's attention. Overusing ね can sound patronizing, especially in formal contexts.

examples.japanese: 今日は天気がいいですね。
examples.romaji: Kyō wa tenki ga ii desu ne.
examples.english: The weather is nice today, isn't it?

examples.japanese: この料理はおいしいですね。
examples.romaji: Kono ryōri wa oishii desu ne.
examples.english: This food is delicious, isn't it?

notes: Compare ね (seeking agreement — you probably already know/feel this) with よ (informing — this is new to you). ね is often used when the speaker assumes shared knowledge; よ is used when giving new information. Together, ねよ softens new information: そうですよね.

name: 〜の
description:
の connects two nouns in a possessive or modifying relationship: 私の本 (my book), 日本語の先生 (Japanese teacher). The first noun modifies or owns the second. It can express possession, origin, material, purpose, or category depending on context.

の also nominalizes verbs and adjectives, turning them into noun phrases: 食べるのが好き (I like eating), 難しいのはこれだ (the difficult one is this). In casual speech, の at the end of a sentence acts as a softening explanatory particle (similar to んです): どうしたの？ (What's wrong?).

examples.japanese: これは私の本です。
examples.romaji: Kore wa watashi no hon desu.
examples.english: This is my book.

examples.japanese: 日本語の勉強は楽しいです。
examples.romaji: Nihongo no benkyō wa tanoshii desu.
examples.english: Studying Japanese is fun.

notes: Unlike English 'of,' Japanese の goes between modifier and modified: 日本の文化 (culture of Japan / Japanese culture). Multiple の can be chained: 私の友達の家 (my friend's house).

name: 〜のだ・〜んだ
description:
のだ (formal) / んだ (spoken contraction) is an explanatory or expressive ending added to plain-form clauses. It has several functions: (1) giving an explanation or justification ("the thing is, ~"), (2) seeking an explanation ("why is it that ~?"), (3) emphasizing a fact with emotional coloring.

In spoken Japanese, のだ contracts to んだ (men) or のです to んです (polite). It is extremely frequent in natural conversation, making it one of the most important structures to master.
usage: Verb (plain) + んだ/のだ / い-adj + んだ / な-adj + なんだ / Noun + なんだ

examples.japanese: 実は、明日引っ越すんです。
examples.romaji: Jitsu wa, ashita hikkosunda desu.
examples.english: The truth is, I'm moving tomorrow.

examples.japanese: どうして遅刻したんですか。
examples.romaji: Doushite chikoku shita ndesu ka.
examples.english: Why is it that you were late?

notes: のだ in questions (〜んですか / のですか) asks for an explanation or shows interest/concern. In statements it provides one. Context distinguishes whether the speaker is explaining or questioning.

name: 〜ので
description:
ので is a reason/cause conjunction meaning "because" or "so". It states an objective, natural reason for the result in the following clause. Compared to から, ので sounds more polite and less assertive, making it the preferred choice in formal contexts.

のでlinks two clauses where the first gives the reason and the second gives the result. Both clauses are by the same speaker, and the reason is presented as a natural, socially accepted explanation.
usage: Verb (plain) + ので / い-adj + ので / な-adj + なので / Noun + なので

examples.japanese: 雨が降っているので、出かけません。
examples.romaji: Ame ga futte iru node, dekakemasen.
examples.english: Since it's raining, I won't go out.

examples.japanese: 試験があるので、勉強しなければなりません。
examples.romaji: Shiken ga aru node, benkyou shinakereba narimasen.
examples.english: Because there is an exam, I have to study.

notes: ので vs から: ので sounds more polite, objective, and natural; から sounds more direct and subjective. In formal apologies or explanations, ので is strongly preferred. Both can begin subordinate clauses.

name: 〜は
description:
The topic marker は (wa) flags what the sentence is about, inviting the listener to direct their attention to that element. Unlike the subject marker が, which highlights who or what performs the action, は presents already-known or contextually established information as the topic and then makes a comment about it — often translated as 'as for X...'.

は can replace が or を in many sentences to add a topicalizing or contrasting nuance. When used in a negative sentence (〜はしない), it frequently implies that while X is not done, something else might be. は is also used to set up contrasts between two things: 猫は好きだが、犬は苦手だ.

examples.japanese: 私は学生です。
examples.romaji: Watashi wa gakusei desu.
examples.english: I am a student.

examples.japanese: これは何ですか？
examples.romaji: Kore wa nan desu ka?
examples.english: What is this?

notes: Beginners often confuse は and が. A helpful rule: が answers 'who/what?' questions and introduces new information; は frames a known topic or sets up a contrast. The kana は is normally read 'ha' but always pronounced 'wa' when used as this particle.

name: 〜へ
description:
へ (e) marks the direction of movement, meaning 'toward' or 'to.' It emphasizes the direction of travel rather than the arrival at a destination. It is largely interchangeable with に for destination when used with motion verbs, but へ has a softer, more directional nuance.

へ is more commonly used in formal writing and set phrases. In everyday speech, に is more natural for destinations. へ cannot mark time or recipients, which distinguishes it from the multi-purpose に.

examples.japanese: 東京へ行きます。
examples.romaji: Tōkyō e ikimasu.
examples.english: I am going to Tokyo.

examples.japanese: 家へ帰りましょう。
examples.romaji: Ie e kaerimashou.
examples.english: Let's go home.

notes: In casual spoken Japanese, に is almost always preferred over へ for destinations. へ sounds slightly more literary or formal. へ is never used with verbs of existence like いる or ある.

name: 〜ほうがいい
description:
ほうがいい is used to give advice or recommendations, meaning "it would be better to ~" or "you should ~". With the past tense (た-form), the advice is stronger and more direct. With the present plain form, it is a gentle suggestion.

The negative equivalent — ないほうがいい — means "you had better not ~" or "it would be better not to ~" and uses the ない-form of the verb.
usage: Verb (た-form) + ほうがいい / Verb (ない-form) + ほうがいい

examples.japanese: 早く病院に行ったほうがいいですよ。
examples.romaji: Hayaku byouin ni itta hou ga ii desu yo.
examples.english: You should go to the hospital soon.

examples.japanese: 遅くまで起きていないほうがいい。
examples.romaji: Osoku made okite inai hou ga ii.
examples.english: You'd better not stay up late.

notes: Using ほうがいい with た-form (past) implies stronger advice ("you really should"), while using it with the dictionary form or ない-form is slightly softer. It is commonly taught as an advice structure.

name: 〜ましょう
description:
ましょう is the volitional form of polite verbs, used to suggest doing something together ("let's ~"). It expresses the speaker's intention or an invitation for joint action, and is one of the first verb forms learned in Japanese.

To form it, drop the ます from the masu-form and add ましょう. It is used in affirmative sentences only — for negative suggestions, use ましょうか with a question intonation or rephrase.

examples.japanese: 一緒に昼ごはんを食べましょう。
examples.romaji: Issho ni hirugohan wo tabemashou.
examples.english: Let's eat lunch together.

examples.japanese: 駅まで歩きましょう。
examples.romaji: Eki made arukimashou.
examples.english: Let's walk to the station.

notes: ましょう is warmer and more inviting than the plain volitional form (行こう). In formal settings such as business or presentations, it is the standard form for group suggestions.

name: 〜ましょうか
description:
ましょうか is the question form of the volitional. It has two distinct uses: (1) offering to do something for the listener ("shall I ~?"), and (2) proposing a joint action ("shall we ~?"). Context and the presence of words like 一緒に determine which meaning is intended.

When used as an offer, the subject is "I" and the listener benefits. When used as a mutual suggestion, it is similar to ましょう but softer because it invites agreement rather than assuming it.

examples.japanese: 荷物を持ちましょうか。
examples.romaji: Nimotsu wo mochimashouka.
examples.english: Shall I carry your luggage?

examples.japanese: そろそろ出発しましょうか。
examples.romaji: Sorosoro shuppatsu shimashouka.
examples.english: Shall we get going soon?

notes: The intonation matters: rising intonation implies a genuine question or offer, while falling intonation leans toward a suggestion. Compare with ませんか, which is an invitation ("won't you ~?").

name: 〜ます
description:
〜ます is the polite verb ending for present/future affirmative tense. It attaches to the verb stem (Group 1: drop the -u and add -i; Group 2: drop -ru). It makes speech polite and formal, appropriate in work, school, and speaking with strangers or superiors.

ます conjugates for tense, negation, and volitional forms: present/future (〜ます), past (〜ました), negative (〜ません), negative past (〜ませんでした), volitional/invitation (〜ましょう), and polite request (〜ませんか). The て-form of ます-verbs attaches to て-form of the verb itself.

examples.japanese: 毎朝コーヒーを飲みます。
examples.romaji: Maiasa kōhī o nomimasu.
examples.english: I drink coffee every morning.

examples.japanese: 明日東京に行きます。
examples.romaji: Ashita Tōkyō ni ikimasu.
examples.english: I will go to Tokyo tomorrow.

notes: The ます-form only applies to verbs; adjectives use です for politeness. In dictionaries, verbs are listed in their plain (dictionary) form, not ます-form. To conjugate, find the verb's group (u-verb / ru-verb / irregular) first.

name: 〜ませんか
description:
ませんか is a polite invitation form meaning "won't you ~?" or "would you like to ~?" It is more deferential than ましょう because it gives the listener the freedom to accept or decline, rather than assuming agreement.

It literally uses the negative polite form plus か, but the meaning is affirmative invitation, not a negative question. It is commonly used to invite someone to do something together.

examples.japanese: 一緒に映画を見に行きませんか。
examples.romaji: Issho ni eiga wo mi ni ikimasenka.
examples.english: Won't you come see a movie with me?

examples.japanese: 週末に公園を散歩しませんか。
examples.romaji: Shuumatsu ni kouen wo sanpo shimasenka.
examples.english: Would you like to take a walk in the park this weekend?

notes: ませんか (invitation) vs ましょうか (offer/suggestion): ませんか asks the listener to participate; ましょうか either proposes an action or offers help.

name: 〜まで
description:
まで marks the endpoint or limit of time, space, or degree — 'until,' 'up to,' 'as far as.' It is the endpoint counterpart to から (from): 9時から5時まで (from 9 to 5). It marks where something continues to: 駅まで歩く (walk to the station).

まで can also mean 'even' in the sense of going so far as: そこまでするの？ (would you go that far?). まで(に) adds a deadline nuance — 'by' a certain time: 月曜日までに提出してください (please submit by Monday). The に changes まで from duration to deadline.

examples.japanese: 五時まで働きます。
examples.romaji: Go-ji made hatarakimasu.
examples.english: I work until five o'clock.

examples.japanese: 駅まで歩いて行きます。
examples.romaji: Eki made aruite ikimasu.
examples.english: I walk to the station.

notes: まで = 'until/up to' (duration continues to that point); までに = 'by' (deadline, action completed before that point). 5時まで勉強する = study until 5; 5時までに提出する = submit by 5.

name: 〜まま
description:
〜まま expresses that something remains in the same state or condition as before — "as is," "leaving it that way," or "while still in that state." It attaches to the た-form of verbs to mean an action was done and the resulting state was left unchanged: 電気をつけたまま寝た (I slept with the lights still on). It can also attach to the ない-form: 宿題をしないまま学校へ行った (I went to school without having done my homework).

With nouns and な-adjectives, まま follows の: 元のまま (as it was originally). The nuance is that something was left undone that perhaps should have been changed.
usage: Verb (た/ない) + まま / Noun + の + まま

examples.japanese: 窓を開けたまま出かけてしまった。
examples.romaji: Mado wo aketa mama dekakete shimatta.
examples.english: I went out leaving the window open.

examples.japanese: 靴を履いたまま家に入らないでください。
examples.romaji: Kutsu wo haita mama ie ni hairanaide kudasai.
examples.english: Please do not enter the house with your shoes still on.

examples.japanese: 何も言わないまま彼は部屋を出た。
examples.romaji: Nani mo iwanai mama kare wa heya wo deta.
examples.english: He left the room without saying anything.

notes: まま differs from ながら: ながら describes two actions happening simultaneously, while まま focuses on a state that persists unchanged.

name: 〜も
description:
も means 'also,' 'too,' or 'even,' adding an element to what has already been stated. When も replaces は or が, it adds the new noun as an additional member of the same category: 私も行く (I'll go too). When も appears with question words (何も、誰も、どこも) and a negative verb, it means 'nothing,' 'nobody,' 'nowhere.'

も can also express an unexpectedly large or small degree: 三時間も待った (waited a whole three hours!). The nuance of surprise or emphasis depends on context.

examples.japanese: 私も学生です。
examples.romaji: Watashi mo gakusei desu.
examples.english: I am also a student.

examples.japanese: 田中さんも来ますか？
examples.romaji: Tanaka-san mo kimasu ka?
examples.english: Will Tanaka-san come too?

notes: も replaces は, が, and を but is added after other particles: に+も → にも, で+も → でも, へ+も → へも. When 何も, 誰も, どこも appear with negative verbs they mean 'nothing/nobody/nowhere'; with positive verbs they mean 'everything/everyone/everywhere.'

name: 〜や〜など
description:
〜や〜など lists nouns in a non-exhaustive way, meaning 'things like A, B, and so on.' Unlike と which lists exhaustively, や implies there are more items not mentioned: 本や雑誌など (books, magazines, and things like that). など (or 等) can appear at the end to reinforce the incomplete nature of the list.

や can be used without など, but adding など makes the non-exhaustive nature even more explicit. This pattern is common in both speech and writing when giving representative examples from a broader category.

examples.japanese: 冷蔵庫にりんごやバナナなどがあります。
examples.romaji: Reizōko ni ringo ya banana nado ga arimasu.
examples.english: There are things like apples and bananas in the fridge.

examples.japanese: 趣味はゲームや読書などです。
examples.romaji: Shumi wa gēmu ya dokusho nado desu.
examples.english: My hobbies are things like gaming and reading.

notes: や = non-exhaustive list of nouns (and things like...). と = exhaustive list (these exact items and no others). In practice, や〜など is one of the most natural ways to give examples in Japanese, equivalent to 'such as' or 'like A and B.'

name: 〜よ
description:
よ is a sentence-final particle that asserts new information to the listener, conveying 'let me tell you,' 'I'm telling you,' or 'you know.' It marks information that the speaker believes the listener does not know yet: もう食べましたよ (I already ate, just so you know).

よ adds confidence and emphasis to statements. It can soften warnings or recommendations: 気をつけてよ (be careful, okay). In casual speech, particularly among women, よ can also add a gentle insistence. Overuse of よ can sound pushy or presumptuous.

examples.japanese: もう遅いですよ。
examples.romaji: Mō osoi desu yo.
examples.english: It's already late, you know.

examples.japanese: あの店はおいしいですよ。
examples.romaji: Ano mise wa oishii desu yo.
examples.english: That restaurant is delicious, I tell you.

notes: よ = new info being asserted (speaker knows, listener doesn't). ね = shared info being confirmed (both already know). Using よ when the listener already knows can seem presumptuous; using ね when you're sharing new info can seem indecisive.

name: 〜ようと思う
description:
〜ようと思う expresses the speaker's intention — "I am thinking of doing / I plan to." It uses the volitional form followed by と思う. Unlike つもりだ (a firm decision), ようと思っている suggests an ongoing intention: 来年日本に行こうと思っている.

The past form ようと思った means "I was thinking of doing." It is one of the most natural ways to express personal plans in Japanese.

examples.japanese: 今年こそダイエットしようと思っている。
examples.romaji: Kotoshi koso daietto shiyou to omotte iru.
examples.english: I am thinking of going on a diet this year for real.

examples.japanese: 卒業したら旅行しようと思います。
examples.romaji: Sotsugyou shitara ryokou shiyou to omoimasu.
examples.english: I am thinking of travelling after I graduate.

examples.japanese: 昨日電話しようと思ったけど、忘れてしまった。
examples.romaji: Kinou denwa shiyou to omotta kedo, wasurete shimatta.
examples.english: I was going to call yesterday, but I forgot.

notes: Use ようと思っている for ongoing intentions and ようと思う for a decision being made right now.

name: 〜を
description:
The direct object marker を (wo/o) marks the noun that receives the action of a transitive verb directly. It is pronounced 'o' in modern Japanese despite the kana を. Every transitive verb — 食べる, 飲む, 読む, 見る — requires its object to be marked with を.

を also marks the space through which motion passes (公園を走る — run through the park) and the point of departure from a location (バスを降りる — get off the bus). These uses extend its meaning beyond simple direct objects.

examples.japanese: ご飯を食べます。
examples.romaji: Gohan o tabemasu.
examples.english: I eat rice.

examples.japanese: 本を読みました。
examples.romaji: Hon o yomimashita.
examples.english: I read a book.

notes: を only appears with transitive verbs. If you are unsure whether a verb takes を, check whether it has a direct 'thing being acted upon.' Intransitive verbs like ある, いる, and なる do not take を.

name: 〜後で
description:
〜後で means 'after doing something' — the action has already been completed when the main event occurs. With a verb, use the た-form (past plain) + 後で: 食べた後で散歩した (took a walk after eating). With a noun: 授業の後で会いましょう (let's meet after class).

The た-form before 後で reflects completion of the first action relative to the second, not the overall tense of the sentence. Compare with 〜てから, which similarly means 'after doing' but emphasizes the sequence more strongly.
usage: Verb (ta) + 後で / Noun + の後で

examples.japanese: 仕事の後で、友達と飲みます。
examples.romaji: Shigoto no ato de, tomodachi to nomimasu.
examples.english: After work, I'll drink with friends.

examples.japanese: 宿題をした後で、ゲームをしていいです。
examples.romaji: Shukudai o shita ato de, gēmu o shite ii desu.
examples.english: After doing your homework, you can play games.

notes: Both 〜た後で and 〜てから mean 'after doing,' but てから implies a direct, intentional sequence (do A, then do B); 後で is more loosely 'at some point after A.' For tightly chained sequences, てから is often preferred.

name: 〜前に
description:
〜前に means 'before doing something' or 'before a certain time/event.' When used with a verb in the dictionary form, it marks the action that comes first: 寝る前に歯を磨く (brush teeth before sleeping). With a noun, it takes の前に: 食事の前に手を洗う (wash hands before meals).

Note that the verb before 前に stays in the dictionary form (non-past), regardless of the tense of the main clause. This is because the verb before 前に describes a relative ordering, not a tense: 昨日、出かける前に電話した (yesterday, before going out, I called).
usage: Verb (dict.) + 前に / Noun + の前に

examples.japanese: 寝る前に歯を磨きます。
examples.romaji: Neru mae ni ha o migakimasu.
examples.english: I brush my teeth before going to bed.

examples.japanese: 食事の前に手を洗ってください。
examples.romaji: Shokuji no mae ni te o aratte kudasai.
examples.english: Please wash your hands before eating.

notes: Contrast with 〜てから (after doing): 食べてから行く (go after eating) vs 食べる前に行く (go before eating). The verb before 前に is always dictionary form; the verb before てから is always て-form.

name: 〜方
description:
〜方 (かた) is attached to the masu-stem of verbs to mean "the way of doing ~" or "how to do ~". It nominalizes the verb phrase, turning it into a noun that refers to the method or manner of the action.

This construction is essential for asking or explaining how things are done. It often pairs with 教える (to teach/explain) or 知る (to know).

examples.japanese: この料理の作り方を教えてください。
examples.romaji: Kono ryouri no tsukurikata wo oshiete kudasai.
examples.english: Please teach me how to make this dish.

examples.japanese: 漢字の書き方がまだわかりません。
examples.romaji: Kanji no kakikata ga mada wakarimasen.
examples.english: I still don't know how to write kanji.

notes: 方 can also mean "direction" (あちらの方 = that direction) or "person" (honorific: 先生の方 = the teacher). In the grammar construction 〜方, it specifically means "method/way" and is always read かた.

name: い形容詞の活用
description:
い-adjectives (イ形容詞) are adjectives whose dictionary form ends in い. They conjugate directly without a copula: plain present (高い), plain negative (高くない), plain past (高かった), plain negative past (高くなかった). The stem is formed by removing the final い.

Before nouns, い-adjectives appear in their dictionary form: 高いビル (a tall building). The te-form (高くて) chains adjectives or gives reason. The adverbial form (高く) modifies verbs: 高く売る (sell at a high price). です can be added for politeness but does not change the adjective's own conjugation.
usage: Adj stem + い / くない / かった / くなかった

examples.japanese: この映画は面白かったです。
examples.romaji: Kono eiga wa omoshirokatta desu.
examples.english: This movie was interesting.

examples.japanese: 昨日はそんなに寒くなかった。
examples.romaji: Kinō wa sonna ni samukunakatta.
examples.english: Yesterday was not that cold.

notes: Do not confuse い-adjectives with な-adjectives that happen to end in い, such as きれい (pretty), きらい (disliked), and ゆうめい (famous). These are な-adjectives despite ending in い and take な before nouns: きれいな花 (NOT きれいい花).

name: でも
description:
でも has two distinct uses. As a conjunction between sentences, it means "but", "however", or "even so" — it connects two contrasting ideas at the start of a new sentence. As a particle attached to nouns, it means "even", "or something", or "any" (e.g. お茶でも = tea or something like that).

The conjunctive でも is used after a complete sentence and begins the next sentence. It is more casual than しかし (however) and is extremely common in everyday speech.

examples.japanese: 疲れています。でも、もう少し頑張ります。
examples.romaji: Tsukarete imasu. Demo, mou sukoshi ganbarimasu.
examples.english: I'm tired. But I'll try a little more.

examples.japanese: お茶でも飲みませんか。
examples.romaji: Ocha demo nomimasenka.
examples.english: Won't you have some tea or something?

notes: The particle でも (noun + でも) implies the item is just one casual option. Compare: お茶を飲む (drink tea, specifically) vs お茶でも飲む (have tea or whatever, casually).

name: て形 (te-form)
description:
The て-form (te-form) is a connective verb form with many uses: chaining sequential actions (食べて寝た — ate then slept), making requests with ください (見せてください — please show me), expressing ongoing states with いる (読んでいる — is reading), granting permission with もいい (行ってもいい — may go), and prohibition with はいけない.

Forming the て-form depends on the verb group: u-verbs undergo sound changes (く→いて, ぐ→いで, す→して, ぬ/む/ぶ→んで, る/つ/う→って); ru-verbs simply drop る and add て; irregular: する→して, くる→きて.

examples.japanese: 手を洗って、ご飯を食べます。
examples.romaji: Te o aratte, gohan o tabemasu.
examples.english: I wash my hands and then eat.

examples.japanese: ちょっと待ってください。
examples.romaji: Chotto matte kudasai.
examples.english: Please wait a moment.

notes: Mastering the te-form is essential because it is the base for dozens of grammar patterns. The て/で distinction (voicing) depends on the ending: verbs ending in ぐ, ぬ, む, ぶ use で; all others use て. Exception: 行く → 行って (not 行いて).

name: どんな
description:
どんな is a pre-nominal question word meaning "what kind of" or "what sort of". It must directly precede a noun and asks about the nature, type, or quality of something. It can be used in both questions and embedded clauses.

Unlike どの (which one among known options), どんな asks about the general character or type without implying a specific set to choose from.

examples.japanese: どんな音楽が好きですか。
examples.romaji: Donna ongaku ga suki desu ka.
examples.english: What kind of music do you like?

examples.japanese: どんな人が来るか分かりません。
examples.romaji: Donna hito ga kuru ka wakarimasen.
examples.english: I don't know what kind of person is coming.

notes: どんな、どの、どれ comparison: どんな = what kind of (type/quality); どの = which (among known items); どれ = which one (of known options). どんな emphasizes qualitative description.

name: な形容詞の活用
description:
な-adjectives (ナ形容詞) attach な before nouns: 静かな部屋 (a quiet room), 便利な駅 (a convenient station). They conjugate with the copula だ/です: plain present (静かだ), plain negative (静かではない/じゃない), plain past (静かだった), polite present (静かです).

The te-form of な-adjectives is formed with で: 静かで (quiet and...), used to chain adjectives or give reason. The adverbial form uses に: 静かに話す (speak quietly). な-adjectives frequently derive from Chinese loanwords or describe states and qualities.
usage: Adj + な + Noun / Adj + だ

examples.japanese: 彼女はとても親切な人です。
examples.romaji: Kanojo wa totemo shinsetsu na hito desu.
examples.english: She is a very kind person.

examples.japanese: この公園は静かではありません。
examples.romaji: Kono kōen wa shizuka dewa arimasen.
examples.english: This park is not quiet.

notes: な-adjectives do not conjugate by themselves — they rely on the copula だ/です for tense and negation. This is the key difference from い-adjectives. Some words can be used as both na-adj and noun: 元気 (genki) — 元気な人 (healthy person) or 元気がある (have energy).

name: まだ
description:
まだ means "still" in affirmative sentences, indicating that a state or action is continuing. In negative sentences, it means "not yet", indicating that an expected action has not happened. It is one of the most fundamental adverbs in Japanese.

With the progressive form (〜ている), まだ + 〜ている means "still doing ~". With a negative verb, まだ + 〜ていない means "haven't done ~ yet".

examples.japanese: 彼女はまだ寝ています。
examples.romaji: Kanojo wa mada nete imasu.
examples.english: She is still sleeping.

examples.japanese: 宿題がまだ終わっていません。
examples.romaji: Shukudai ga mada owatte imasen.
examples.english: I haven't finished my homework yet.

notes: Contrast: まだ (still/not yet) vs もう (already/anymore). まだ〜ている = still doing; もう〜た = already done; もう〜ない = not anymore. These four combinations are tested frequently at N5.

name: もう
description:
もう has two related meanings depending on polarity. In affirmative sentences, it means "already" — the action is complete. In negative sentences, it means "not anymore" or "no longer" — the state has ceased.

もう can also express frustration or exasperation (もう！= Ugh! / Enough!), and in some contexts means "another" or "one more" (もう一つ = one more).

examples.japanese: もう食べました。
examples.romaji: Mou tabemashita.
examples.english: I already ate.

examples.japanese: もう疲れた。もうできない。
examples.romaji: Mou tsukareta. Mou dekinai.
examples.english: I'm already tired. I can't do it anymore.

notes: もう vs まだ: もう〜た = already done; まだ〜ていない = not done yet. もう〜ない = no longer ~; まだ〜ている = still ~. Also: もう少し (a little more), もう一度 (one more time).

name: 一番〜
description:
一番 (いちばん) means "the most", "the best", or "number one". It is placed before adjectives or adverbs to form the superlative degree in Japanese. Japanese does not inflect adjectives for superlatives; instead, 一番 is added.

It can also stand alone to mean "first" or "best" in general rankings. In questions, it appears with の中で (among ~) to specify the comparison group.
usage: 一番 + い-adj / 一番 + な-adj (な) + Noun

examples.japanese: クラスの中で田中さんが一番背が高い。
examples.romaji: Kurasu no naka de Tanaka-san ga ichiban se ga takai.
examples.english: Tanaka is the tallest in the class.

examples.japanese: 日本で一番有名な山は富士山です。
examples.romaji: Nihon de ichiban yuumei na yama wa Fujisan desu.
examples.english: The most famous mountain in Japan is Mt. Fuji.

notes: Superlative comparisons often use 〜の中で一番〜 (the most ~ among/in ~). For "least", use 一番〜ない or 最も〜ない. 最も (mottomo) is the more formal/literary equivalent.

name: 〜あまり〜ない
description:
あまり〜ない means "not very ~", "not much", or "not all that ~". It is used to express that something is below a normal or expected degree. The negative verb or adjective follows あまり.

Note the difference from the N3 usage of あまり (without negation), which means "so much that / too ~" (leading to an unexpected result). At N4, the pattern always requires a negative predicate.
usage: あまり + Verb (negative) / あまり + い-adj + くない / あまり + な-adj + ではない

examples.japanese: 最近あまり運動していません。
examples.romaji: Saikin amari undou shite imasen.
examples.english: I haven't been exercising much lately.

examples.japanese: この映画はあまり面白くなかった。
examples.romaji: Kono eiga wa amari omoshirokunakatta.
examples.english: This movie wasn't very interesting.

notes: あまり vs 全然(ぜんぜん): あまり〜ない = not very/much (partial negation); 全然〜ない = not at all (total negation). あまりにも (N3) = excessively, too much — used with affirmative predicates.

name: 〜うちに
description:
うちに means "while ~" or "before it's too late". It indicates that the action in the main clause should happen or happens within the time frame of the preceding condition. There is often an implication that after that time frame ends, the opportunity will be lost.

With affirmative verbs and adjectives, it means "while the state holds". With negative forms, it means "before ~ happens" (i.e., while it has not yet happened). The nuance often includes urgency.
usage: Verb (plain present) + うちに / い-adj + うちに / な-adj + な + うちに / Noun + の + うちに

examples.japanese: 熱いうちに食べてください。
examples.romaji: Atsui uchi ni tabete kudasai.
examples.english: Please eat it while it's hot.

examples.japanese: 忘れないうちにメモしておきました。
examples.romaji: Wasurenai uchi ni memo shite okimashita.
examples.english: I wrote it down before I forgot.

notes: うちに often carries a sense of "seize the moment while the condition still allows it". Compare: 〜間に (aida ni) which simply states two simultaneous events without urgency. うちに implies the window will close.

name: 〜かい
description:
かい is an informal question particle used mainly in casual masculine speech. It turns a statement into a yes/no question with a softer, more intimate tone than か. It is often used by older men or characters in fiction and can sound warm or slightly blunt depending on context.

かい appears after plain-form predicates. It is less common in standard modern speech but is frequently encountered in literature, manga, and traditional settings.
usage: Verb (plain) + かい / い-adj + かい / Noun (plain) + かい

examples.japanese: 今日も仕事かい？
examples.romaji: Kyou mo shigoto kai?
examples.english: Working today too?

examples.japanese: もう食べたかい？
examples.romaji: Mou tabeta kai?
examples.english: Did you eat already?

notes: かい is mainly used by middle-aged to older male speakers in casual settings. It is softer and more intimate than か. Female equivalent in similar casual register: の (ね)? The particle is not appropriate in formal or business contexts.

name: 〜かしら
description:
かしら is a sentence-final particle meaning "I wonder ~" or "I'm not sure ~". It expresses mild doubt, curiosity, or uncertainty. Traditionally associated with feminine speech, it appears at the end of plain-form statements or questions.

While かしら is often labeled as feminine, it is increasingly used by speakers of any gender in certain contexts, particularly in written or literary registers. Its male equivalent is かな.
usage: Verb (plain) + かしら / い-adj + かしら / Noun + かしら

examples.japanese: もう電車が来たかしら。
examples.romaji: Mou densha ga kita kashira.
examples.english: I wonder if the train has come already.

examples.japanese: 彼は私のことが好きかしら。
examples.romaji: Kare wa watashi no koto ga suki kashira.
examples.english: I wonder if he likes me.

notes: かしら vs かな: both express "I wonder ~". かしら is traditionally feminine; かな is more gender-neutral/masculine. Both can be used in rhetorical questions where the speaker is musing, not actually asking.

name: 〜かどうか
description:
かどうか means "whether or not ~". It embeds a yes/no question as a noun clause, which can then function as the subject or object of the sentence. It is the standard way to express uncertainty or indirect yes/no questions in Japanese.

かどうか is more specific than か〜か (A or B?) because it always involves a single fact with two possible answers: yes or no. The clause before かどうか is always in plain form.
usage: Verb (plain) + かどうか / い-adj + かどうか / な-adj + かどうか / Noun + かどうか

examples.japanese: 彼が来るかどうか分かりません。
examples.romaji: Kare ga kuru ka dou ka wakarimasen.
examples.english: I don't know whether or not he'll come.

examples.japanese: 合格できるかどうか、心配しています。
examples.romaji: Goukaku dekiru ka dou ka, shinpai shite imasu.
examples.english: I'm worried about whether or not I can pass.

notes: かどうか can be simplified to か when the alternative is obviously its opposite: 行くか分かりません (I don't know if I'll go). かどうか is more explicit and clearer in formal writing. Compare か〜か (A or B, two stated alternatives).

name: 〜かな
description:
かな is a sentence-final particle meaning "I wonder ~" or expressing mild uncertainty, speculation, or a soft question to oneself. Unlike かしら, it is gender-neutral and widely used in contemporary Japanese by all speakers.

かな can also express a desire or hope in indirect way: 行けるかな (I wonder if I can go / I hope I can go). It softens statements and questions, making them sound thoughtful rather than demanding.
usage: Verb (plain) + かな / い-adj + かな / な-adj + かな / Noun + かな

examples.japanese: 明日は晴れるかな。
examples.romaji: Ashita wa hareru kana.
examples.english: I wonder if it will be sunny tomorrow.

examples.japanese: 彼女、元気かな。
examples.romaji: Kanojo, genki kana.
examples.english: I wonder if she's doing well.

notes: かな can be used in rhetorical questions where the speaker muses aloud. It is distinct from か (direct question to listener) and かしら (similar meaning but traditionally feminine). かなあ (elongated) makes the wondering even more open-ended.

name: 〜かもしれない
description:
〜かもしれない expresses possibility — 'might,' 'maybe,' 'perhaps.' It attaches to the plain form of verbs and adjectives, and to nouns/な-adj without だ: 雨が降るかもしれない (it might rain), 彼は病気かもしれない (he might be sick). It conveys uncertainty — the speaker is not confident about the statement.

かもしれない is less certain than はずだ (logical expectation) but more possible than the speaker simply guessing. In casual speech it contracts to かも: そうかも (might be). In formal contexts, かもしれません is used.
usage: Verb / Adj (plain) + かもしれない

examples.japanese: 明日は雨が降るかもしれません。
examples.romaji: Ashita wa ame ga furu kamoshiremasen.
examples.english: It might rain tomorrow.

examples.japanese: 財布をどこかに落としたかもしれない。
examples.romaji: Saifu o dokoka ni otoshita kamoshirenai.
examples.english: I might have dropped my wallet somewhere.

notes: Certainty spectrum (high→low): 〜に違いない (must be, I'm certain) → 〜はずだ (should be, logically expected) → 〜だろう (probably) → 〜かもしれない (might) → 〜とは思えない (can't imagine). Use かもしれない when you're genuinely uncertain.

name: 〜がる・〜がっている
description:
がる is attached to the stems of emotion-expressing adjectives (い-adj or な-adj) to mean "to show signs of feeling ~", "to seem to feel ~", or "to act as if ~". It turns a subjective feeling into an observable behavior.

Because Japanese considers it presumptuous to directly state another person's internal feelings, がる is used when describing how others appear to feel. The progressive form がっている describes an ongoing observable state. First-person use is rare.
usage: い-adj (remove い) + がる / な-adj stem + がる

examples.japanese: 弟は新しいゲームをほしがっている。
examples.romaji: Otouto wa atarashii geemu wo hoshigatte iru.
examples.english: My younger brother seems to want a new game.

examples.japanese: 彼女は発表をとても怖がっていた。
examples.romaji: Kanojo wa happyou wo totemo kowagatte ita.
examples.english: She seemed very scared of the presentation.

notes: Common adjective + がる: 欲しがる (seem to want), 怖がる (seem to be scared), 嫌がる (seem to dislike), 悲しがる (seem sad), 喜ぶ is already a verb so doesn't use がる. がり as a noun means "tendency" (寂しがり = lonely type).

name: 〜ことがある (non-past)
description:
ことがある with a non-past (dictionary form) verb means "there are times when ~" or "sometimes ~". It expresses that a habitual or occasional event occurs. This differs from the past-tense version (たことがある), which expresses past experience ("have done ~").

The subject can be the speaker or a third party. The action happens sometimes, not regularly. For regular habits, use 〜ことが多い or adverbs like よく and たまに.

examples.japanese: 仕事が忙しくて、食事を抜くことがある。
examples.romaji: Shigoto ga isogashikute, shokuji wo nuku koto ga aru.
examples.english: There are times when I skip meals because work is busy.

examples.japanese: 電車で寝過ごすことがあります。
examples.romaji: Densha de nesugosou koto ga arimasu.
examples.english: Sometimes I oversleep on the train.

notes: Dictionary form + ことがある (habitual: sometimes ~) vs た-form + ことがある (experiential: have ~ before). 行くことがある = sometimes go vs 行ったことがある = have been there. The tense of ことがある itself can also change: ことがあった = there used to be times when ~.

name: 〜ごろ
description:
ごろ indicates an approximate time or period, meaning "around ~", "about ~", or "approximately (time)". It is used with time expressions — hours, dates, periods — to show that the time given is not exact.

ごろ can only be used with time expressions, not quantities or distances (use くらい/ぐらい for those). It expresses vagueness about a point in time, not a duration (for durations, use ぐらい).

examples.japanese: 三時ごろ着きます。
examples.romaji: Sanji goro tsukimasu.
examples.english: I'll arrive around three o'clock.

examples.japanese: 来年ごろ、引っ越す予定です。
examples.romaji: Rainen goro, hikkuosu yotei desu.
examples.english: I plan to move around next year.

notes: ごろ vs ぐらい/くらい: ごろ = around a point in time (三時ごろ = around 3pm); ぐらい = approximately a quantity or duration (三時間ぐらい = about 3 hours). ごろ cannot be used with quantities.

name: 〜しか〜ない
description:
〜しか〜ない means 'nothing but,' 'only' — and always requires a negative verb. The structure restricts to a single option with an implied sense that it's not enough or that no alternative exists: 百円しかない (I only have 100 yen — and that's not much). The negativity of the verb doesn't make the sentence negative in meaning; rather, the overall meaning is positive but limited.

しか replaces は, が, or を but combines with other particles: にしか, でしか, からしか. The nuance is stronger than だけ — しか implies the limitation is noteworthy, often insufficient, or exclusive.

examples.japanese: 財布に百円しかありません。
examples.romaji: Saifu ni hyakuen shika arimasen.
examples.english: I only have 100 yen in my wallet.

examples.japanese: あの店には現金しか使えない。
examples.romaji: Ano mise ni wa genkin shika tsukaenai.
examples.english: You can only use cash at that store.

notes: Always use a negative verb with しか: 〜しかない, 〜しかしない, 〜しか食べない. Using しか with a positive verb is ungrammatical. The negative verb doesn't negate the meaning — 水しか飲まない means 'I only drink water' (positive meaning, negative verb).

name: 〜そうだ (appearance)
description:
〜そうだ (appearance/conjecture) expresses that something 'looks like' or 'seems' a certain way, based on direct visual or sensory evidence. It attaches to: verb stems (降りそう — looks like it's going to rain), い-adj stems removing い (おいしそう — looks delicious), な-adj stems (元気そう — looks healthy).

This そうだ is based on the speaker's immediate perception or observation. It differs from the hearsay そうだ (which follows a full clause). Negative visual inference: 降りそうもない (doesn't look like it will rain). The adjectival そうな can modify nouns: おいしそうなケーキ (a cake that looks delicious).
usage: Verb stem / Adj stem + そうだ

examples.japanese: この料理はおいしそうです。
examples.romaji: Kono ryōri wa oishisō desu.
examples.english: This food looks delicious.

examples.japanese: 空が暗くなってきた。雨が降りそうだ。
examples.romaji: Sora ga kuraku natte kita. Ame ga furisō da.
examples.english: The sky has gotten dark. It looks like it's going to rain.

notes: Visual そうだ (stem + そう) vs hearsay そうだ (plain clause + そう): 雨が降りそうだ (it looks like rain — I see dark clouds) vs 雨が降るそうだ (I heard it will rain — someone told me). The attachment point is the key difference: stem vs full plain form.

name: 〜そうだ (hearsay)
description:
〜そうだ (hearsay) reports information heard from others or from media — 'I hear that,' 'apparently,' 'they say.' Unlike the visual そうだ (which uses a stem), this hearsay そうだ attaches to the full plain form: 明日は雨が降るそうだ (apparently it will rain tomorrow — I heard this somewhere).

The source of information is external to the speaker. It is used to relay news or rumors without asserting personal knowledge: 田中さんは引っ越したそうだ (I hear Tanaka-san moved). In writing it appears as によると〜そうだ: ニュースによると、地震があったそうだ (according to the news, there was an earthquake).

examples.japanese: 来週から雨が続くそうです。
examples.romaji: Raishū kara ame ga tsuzuku sō desu.
examples.english: I heard that it will keep raining from next week.

examples.japanese: 田中さんは来月結婚するそうだ。
examples.romaji: Tanaka-san wa raigetsu kekkon suru sō da.
examples.english: Apparently, Tanaka-san is getting married next month.

notes: Hearsay そうだ (plain form + そうだ) vs visual/conjecture そうだ (stem + そうだ): 雨が降るそうだ (I heard it will rain) vs 雨が降りそうだ (it looks like it will rain). The attachment point — plain form vs stem — is the key distinguishing feature.

name: 〜だけ
description:
〜だけ limits quantity or scope — 'only,' 'just,' 'merely.' It follows nouns, plain verbs, or adjectives: これだけ (just this), できるだけ (as much as possible), 一人だけ (just one person). It restricts the statement to what is mentioned without implying anything missing from an expected amount.

だけ can attach to various word types and positions. Unlike しか (which requires a negative verb), だけ can appear with positive verbs: 少しだけ食べた (ate just a little). だけあって is a set phrase meaning 'as expected of / no wonder': さすがプロだけあって (as expected of a professional...).

examples.japanese: 少しだけ食べました。
examples.romaji: Sukoshi dake tabemashita.
examples.english: I only ate a little.

examples.japanese: ここだけの話ですが…
examples.romaji: Koko dake no hanashi desu ga…
examples.english: This is just between us, but…

notes: だけ vs しか: both mean 'only,' but しか always requires a negative verb. 一つだけある (there is just one) vs 一つしかない (there is only one — implies not enough). しか carries a stronger nuance of insufficiency or limitation; だけ is more neutral.

name: 〜だって
description:
だって is a versatile casual particle with several uses: (1) "because" or "but" — giving a reason or excuse casually (だって、知らなかったんだもん = But I didn't know!); (2) "even ~" as a particle (子どもだってわかる = even a child would understand); (3) hearsay marker meaning "they say" or "I heard that".

It is predominantly used in casual speech and often has a slightly defensive, childlike, or pleading tone when used as "because". As "even", it follows nouns and is equivalent to 〜でも in particle use.
usage: だって + [reason/excuse] / Noun + だって + [fact]

examples.japanese: だって、誰も教えてくれなかったから。
examples.romaji: Datte, dare mo oshiete kurenakatta kara.
examples.english: But nobody told me, so...

examples.japanese: その問題は子どもだってわかる。
examples.romaji: Sono mondai wa kodomo datte wakaru.
examples.english: Even a child can understand that problem.

notes: だって as a reason/excuse is typically used by children or in casual/informal speech and sounds slightly petulant. As "even", it is interchangeable with でも in many cases. As hearsay, compare with って: どちらも "I heard that~" in casual speech.

name: 〜ために
description:
〜ために has two main uses. As a purpose marker with volitional verbs, it means 'in order to': 日本語を上手になるために、毎日練習する (I practice every day in order to get better at Japanese). As a cause marker, it expresses reason — 'because of,' 'due to': 台風のために、試合が中止になった (the match was cancelled due to the typhoon).

The key distinction: purpose ために follows a dictionary-form verb with a volitional actor; cause ために follows a noun (+ の) or a plain-form verb/adjective describing a state beyond the speaker's control. The context usually makes the meaning clear.
usage: Verb (dict.) + ために / Noun + の + ために

examples.japanese: 日本語を上手になるために、毎日練習します。
examples.romaji: Nihongo o jōzu ni naru tame ni, mainichi renshū shimasu.
examples.english: I practice every day in order to get better at Japanese.

examples.japanese: 家族のために一生懸命働いています。
examples.romaji: Kazoku no tame ni isshoukenmei hataraite imasu.
examples.english: I am working hard for my family.

notes: Purpose ために vs. cause/reason ために: 大学に入るために勉強する (study in order to enter university — purpose) vs 事故のために電車が遅れた (the train was delayed due to an accident — cause). The cause usage often involves external, uncontrollable events.

name: 〜たら
description:
〜たら is a versatile conditional meaning 'when,' 'if,' or 'after.' It is formed by adding ら to the た-form of any verb or adjective: 食べたら (when/after eating), 雨だったら (if it rains). It covers hypothetical conditions, temporal sequences, and discovery.

たら can express: (1) simple 'if': もし負けたら、どうする？ (what will you do if you lose?); (2) temporal 'when/after': 帰ったら、電話して (call when you get home); (3) discovery: 開けてみたら、何もなかった (when I opened it, there was nothing). It is the most versatile conditional.

examples.japanese: 家に帰ったら、すぐ電話してください。
examples.romaji: Ie ni kaettara, sugu denwa shite kudasai.
examples.english: When you get home, please call me right away.

examples.japanese: 宝くじが当たったら、何をしますか？
examples.romaji: Takarakuji ga atattara, nani o shimasu ka?
examples.english: If you won the lottery, what would you do?

notes: たら is the most flexible Japanese conditional and safest to use when unsure. It is the only conditional that can naturally appear with commands in the result clause: 着いたら連絡してね (let me know when you arrive). If the condition and result relate to the same moment of discovery, たら is almost always correct.

name: 〜たらどうですか
description:
たらどうですか is used to make suggestions, meaning "why don't you ~?", "how about ~?", or "wouldn't it be a good idea to ~?". It is a gentle recommendation that gives the listener the choice to follow the advice or not.

The casual form is たらどう (dropping ですか), or even just たら (dropping どうですか entirely) in very casual speech. The negative form たらどうですか is less common but possible.

examples.japanese: 少し休んだらどうですか。
examples.romaji: Sukoshi yasundara dou desu ka.
examples.english: Why don't you take a short rest?

examples.japanese: 医者に相談したらどうでしょうか。
examples.romaji: Isha ni soudan shitara dou deshou ka.
examples.english: How about consulting a doctor?

notes: たらどうですか vs ほうがいい: both give advice, but たらどうですか is a softer suggestion framed as a question; ほうがいい is a more direct recommendation ("you should"). たらどうですか implies the speaker respects the listener's autonomy.

name: 〜って
description:
って is a casual spoken particle with several uses: (1) quotation marker (casual equivalent of と) meaning "they said that ~" or "it says ~"; (2) a topic marker (casual は) re-emphasizing a topic; (3) a shortened form of という, meaning "called ~" or "that thing called ~".

As a quotation marker, って comes after a clause in plain form and reports speech or thoughts casually. It is one of the most important particles in natural spoken Japanese.
usage: Clause (plain) + って (quotation) / Noun + って (topic/definition)

examples.japanese: 田中さんが明日来るって言っていました。
examples.romaji: Tanaka-san ga ashita kuru tte itte imashita.
examples.english: Tanaka said he's coming tomorrow.

examples.japanese: 「愛」って何？
examples.romaji: "Ai" tte nani?
examples.english: What is "love"?

notes: って (quotation) vs と言う: って is casual; と言う is standard. って (topic) vs は: って re-introduces or emphasizes a topic conversationally. In rapid speech, って can even precede verbs of saying directly: 来るって (He says he's coming).

name: 〜つもりだ
description:
〜つもりだ expresses a firm personal intention or plan — 'I intend to,' 'I plan to.' It attaches to the dictionary form: 来年、日本に行くつもりだ (I intend to go to Japan next year). It implies a more decided, internal plan compared to the vaguer 〜と思っている.

The negative form 〜ないつもりだ means 'I intend not to': もう食べないつもりだ (I intend not to eat anymore). つもりだった expresses an unfulfilled intention: 早く起きるつもりだったが、起きられなかった (I had intended to wake up early, but couldn't).

examples.japanese: 来年、日本に留学するつもりです。
examples.romaji: Rainen, Nihon ni ryūgaku suru tsumori desu.
examples.english: I plan to study abroad in Japan next year.

examples.japanese: タバコをやめるつもりだ。
examples.romaji: Tabako o yameru tsumori da.
examples.english: I intend to quit smoking.

notes: つもりだ expresses the speaker's own firmly held intention. It sounds strange when used for others' intentions — use だろう or でしょう for third persons. つもりだった (past) is useful for 'I had planned to but...' expressions of broken plans.

name: 〜てあげる
description:
〜てあげる describes doing a favor for someone — the speaker or an in-group member does something for someone outside the group. The beneficiary is typically marked with に: 友達に本を貸してあげた (I lent a book to my friend as a favor). It implies the speaker is conferring a benefit on the recipient.

Be careful with the direction of giving: あげる flows outward (I → you, I → them). The opposite direction — receiving a favor — uses くれる (they → me/in-group) or もらう (I receive from them). Overusing あげる for things you do for superiors can sound condescending; use 〜させていただく instead.

examples.japanese: 友達に日本語を教えてあげました。
examples.romaji: Tomodachi ni Nihongo o oshiete agemashita.
examples.english: I taught Japanese to my friend (as a favor).

examples.japanese: 荷物を持ってあげましょうか？
examples.romaji: Nimotsu o motte agemashou ka?
examples.english: Shall I carry your luggage for you?

notes: Using てあげる for something you do for a superior sounds patronizing in Japanese. When doing something for a boss or elder, say 〜します or 〜させていただきます instead. てあげる is best reserved for peers, family, or those below you in social hierarchy.

name: 〜ていく
description:
〜ていく describes a gradual change or continuation moving away from the present into the future — 'will continue to' or 'will progressively do.' It can also describe doing something and leaving: コーヒーを買っていく (buy coffee and go / take it with me as I go).

ていく uses the movement verb いく (to go) metaphorically to indicate movement away from the current moment or location. Paired with change verbs, it expresses an ongoing trend: 人口が増えていく (the population will continue to grow).

examples.japanese: これからもっと練習していきます。
examples.romaji: Korekara motto renshū shite ikimasu.
examples.english: I will continue practicing more from now on.

examples.japanese: 人口はこれから減っていくだろう。
examples.romaji: Jinkō wa korekara hette iku darō.
examples.english: The population will probably continue to decline going forward.

notes: Contrast てくる (approaching the present) and ていく (moving away from the present). 経済が回復してきた = the economy has been recovering (up to now); 経済が回復していく = the economy will continue to recover (into the future).

name: 〜ておく
description:
〜ておく means to do something in advance or to leave something in a certain state deliberately, with future benefit or preparation in mind: 旅行の前に地図を調べておく (look up the map in advance before the trip). The action is done proactively so it is ready when needed.
