ておく also means to leave something as is intentionally: そのままにしておいて (leave it as it is). In casual speech, ておく contracts to 〜とく: 買っとく (buy it in advance / buy ahead). The nuance is always purposeful — the action is done with a future purpose in mind.

examples.japanese: 旅行の前にホテルを予約しておきました。
examples.romaji: Ryokō no mae ni hoteru o yoyaku shite okimashita.
examples.english: I booked a hotel in advance before the trip.

examples.japanese: パーティーのために食べ物を買っておいた。
examples.romaji: Pātī no tame ni tabemono o katte oita.
examples.english: I bought food ahead of time for the party.

notes: 〜ておく (preparing for the future) is different from 〜てある (a state resulting from someone's action): 窓を開けておいた (I opened the window in advance) vs 窓が開けてある (the window is open — someone opened it). ておく focuses on the actor's intention; てある focuses on the resultant state.

name: 〜てくる
description:
〜てくる describes a change or development that has been happening up to the present moment — 'has been doing and continues to now.' It can express: (1) gradual change approaching the present: だんだん暗くなってきた (it has gradually gotten darker); (2) doing something and coming back: 買ってくる (go buy it and come back).

てくる uses the movement verb くる (to come) metaphorically to indicate movement toward the present or toward the speaker. The 'going and returning' usage is very common in daily life: ちょっと出かけてくる (I'm going out for a bit and coming back).

examples.japanese: だんだん寒くなってきました。
examples.romaji: Dandan samuku natte kimashita.
examples.english: It has gradually become cold.

examples.japanese: コンビニで買い物をしてきます。
examples.romaji: Konbini de kaimono o shite kimasu.
examples.english: I'll go do some shopping at the convenience store (and come back).

notes: てくる (toward present/speaker) vs ていく (away from present/speaker). Both can express gradual change: 寒くなってきた (getting colder — approaching now) vs 暑くなっていく (getting hotter — moving into the future). The directionality is metaphorical.

name: 〜てくれる
description:
〜てくれる describes someone doing a favor for the speaker or the speaker's in-group. The subject of くれる is always someone outside the speaker's in-group: 友達が手伝ってくれた (my friend helped me — what a nice thing). It expresses gratitude or appreciation for the action received.

The direction of くれる is always inward — from outside the group toward the speaker: they → me/us. Contrast with もらう, which shifts perspective to 'I receive': 友達に手伝ってもらった (I had my friend help me). Both express the same event but from different viewpoints.

examples.japanese: 母が料理を作ってくれました。
examples.romaji: Haha ga ryōri o tsukutte kuremashita.
examples.english: My mother cooked for me.

examples.japanese: 田中さんが道を教えてくれました。
examples.romaji: Tanaka-san ga michi o oshiete kuremashita.
examples.english: Tanaka-san told me the way.

notes: てくれる and てもらう describe the same exchange but from opposite perspectives. てくれる focuses on the giver's action (they did it for me); てもらう focuses on the receiver's experience (I had it done for me). In English both translate roughly the same way.

name: 〜てしまう
description:
〜てしまう expresses completion of an action, often with a nuance of regret, dismay, or unintentional consequence: 財布を忘れてしまった (I accidentally forgot my wallet — what a pain). It can also express simple emphatic completion without strong regret: 宿題をやってしまおう (let me just get the homework done).

In casual spoken Japanese, てしまう contracts: 〜てしまう → 〜ちゃう (plain), 〜てしまった → 〜ちゃった, and for voiced endings でしまう → 〜じゃう. These contractions are very common in conversation.

examples.japanese: 財布を忘れてしまいました。
examples.romaji: Saifu o wasurete shimaimashita.
examples.english: I accidentally left my wallet behind.

examples.japanese: 宿題をやってしまった。
examples.romaji: Shukudai o yatte shimatta.
examples.english: I went ahead and finished the homework.

notes: The regret nuance depends on context. 食べてしまった can mean 'I ate it all (regrettably)' or just 'I went ahead and ate it.' The contraction ちゃう/じゃう is extremely common in casual speech — mastering it is key to sounding natural.

name: 〜てみる
description:
〜てみる means 'to try doing' — attempting an action to see what it's like or what the result will be: 食べてみる (try eating it / eat it and see), 聞いてみよう (let me try asking). The nuance is experimental — you're doing something to find out the outcome.

てみる attaches to the て-form and conjugates like みる: てみた (tried), てみます (will try), てみたい (want to try). It is one of the most common grammar patterns in Japanese and extremely useful for expressing willingness to try new things.

examples.japanese: この料理を食べてみてください。
examples.romaji: Kono ryōri o tabete mite kudasai.
examples.english: Please try eating this dish.

examples.japanese: 新しい方法でやってみましょう。
examples.romaji: Atarashii hōhō de yatte mimashou.
examples.english: Let's try doing it a new way.

notes: 〜てみる implies uncertainty about the result — you're testing something. If you already know the outcome, みる is not used. Compare: ちょっと食べてみる (let me try a bite to see) vs 食べる (I will eat it, no experimentation implied).

name: 〜てもらう
description:
〜てもらう expresses receiving a favor — the speaker arranges for or has someone do something for them. It shifts perspective to the receiver: 友達に翻訳してもらった (I had my friend translate it / I got my friend to translate it). The person who does the action is marked with に.

てもらう implies the speaker benefited from the action. It is also used to express indirect requests: 書いてもらえますか (could you write it for me?). The potential form てもらえる makes a polite request. Compare with てくれる, which is identical in event but focuses on the giver rather than the receiver.

examples.japanese: 先生に作文を直してもらいました。
examples.romaji: Sensei ni sakubun o naoshite moraimashita.
examples.english: I had my teacher correct my composition.

examples.japanese: 友達に写真を撮ってもらいました。
examples.romaji: Tomodachi ni shashin o totte moraimashita.
examples.english: I had my friend take a photo of me.

notes: 〜てもらう is often used to make polite, indirect requests: 〜てもらえますか / 〜てもらえませんか. This is frequently more polite than a direct imperative. The person performing the favor is marked with に (not が): 先生に説明してもらった.

name: 〜と (conditional)
description:
〜と as a conditional means 'when/if (X happens, Y always results)' — it expresses a natural, automatic, or inevitable consequence. The result follows from the condition without exception: 春になると、桜が咲く (when spring comes, the cherry blossoms bloom). It is commonly used for natural laws, habitual truths, and instructions.

と attaches to the dictionary form of verbs and adjectives. Crucially, the result clause cannot express commands, requests, or volitional actions — it must be a natural result. This restricts its use compared to たら or ば.

examples.japanese: 右に曲がると、銀行があります。
examples.romaji: Migi ni magaru to, ginkō ga arimasu.
examples.english: If you turn right, there is a bank.

examples.japanese: 春になると、暖かくなる。
examples.romaji: Haru ni naru to, atatakaku naru.
examples.english: When spring comes, it gets warm.

notes: The result clause of 〜と cannot contain commands (〜て), requests (〜てください), volitional forms (〜よう), or expressions of intention. Use たら for those cases. The と conditional is perfect for instructions: このボタンを押すと、ドアが開く (press this button and the door opens).

name: 〜として
description:
として means "as ~", "in the capacity of ~", or "in the role of ~". It indicates the status, role, or position in which the subject performs an action. It follows nouns and assigns them a specific function or identity in a given context.

として can also appear as としては ("as for ~"), としても ("even as ~"), or としての ("as a, which is ~"). These extended forms are common at N3–N2 level.

examples.japanese: 彼はリーダーとして活躍しています。
examples.romaji: Kare wa riidaa toshite katsuyaku shite imasu.
examples.english: He is active as a leader.

examples.japanese: 留学生として日本語を勉強しています。
examples.romaji: Ryuugakusei toshite Nihongo wo benkyou shite imasu.
examples.english: I am studying Japanese as an international student.

notes: として is often confused with で (instrumental): として emphasizes role or capacity; で emphasizes the means or place. 医者として働く (work as a doctor, in that capacity) vs 医者で働く (work at a doctor's/hospital as a place).

name: 〜と思う
description:
〜と思う expresses the speaker's opinion, belief, or conjecture — 'I think that,' 'I believe that.' The plain form of the verb/adjective/noun + だ precedes と思う: 明日は晴れると思う (I think it will be sunny tomorrow). It is one of the most common ways to express personal opinions in Japanese.

To express ongoing thought or a current state of mind, 〜と思っている is used: 転職しようと思っている (I'm thinking of changing jobs). The past form 〜と思った expresses what one used to think or a sudden realization: あ、そうかと思った (ah, I thought so).

examples.japanese: 彼は正しいと思います。
examples.romaji: Kare wa tadashii to omoimasu.
examples.english: I think he is right.

examples.japanese: 明日は雨が降ると思う。
examples.romaji: Ashita wa ame ga furu to omou.
examples.english: I think it will rain tomorrow.

notes: 〜と思う (I think/believe) softens statements and is essential for expressing opinions without sounding too direct. In formal writing or speech, 〜と考える (consider) is more appropriate. Note: 〜と思います is polite; 〜と思う is plain/casual.

name: 〜ながら
description:
〜ながら expresses two simultaneous actions performed by the same subject — 'while doing X, also doing Y.' The subordinate action (the one in ながら) is typically the background activity: 音楽を聴きながら勉強する (study while listening to music). The main action comes after.

ながら attaches to the verb stem. The subject of both clauses must be the same person. It cannot be used when the two actions cannot literally happen at the same time (e.g., you cannot sleep and run simultaneously). The background action is usually less important than the main verb.
usage: Verb stem + ながら + main action

examples.japanese: 音楽を聴きながら勉強します。
examples.romaji: Ongaku o kikinagara benkyō shimasu.
examples.english: I study while listening to music.

examples.japanese: 歩きながら電話するのは危ない。
examples.romaji: Aruki nagara denwa suru no wa abunai.
examples.english: Talking on the phone while walking is dangerous.

notes: ながら requires the SAME subject for both actions. If subjects differ, use 〜ている間に or 〜ている時に instead. Also, ながら implies the actions are truly simultaneous, not sequential — for 'after,' use てから or 後で.

name: 〜なくて
description:
なくて is the て-form of the negative ない, used to connect clauses. It has two main uses: (1) expressing a reason or cause in negative form ("because ~ not ~"), and (2) listing negative states in sequence (noun/adjective contexts: 忙しくなくて = not being busy, and ~).

For actions and verbs, the sequential meaning (did not do A, then did B) is more typically expressed by 〜ないで. なくて tends to be used for causes/reasons or for adjective/noun negations.
usage: Verb (ない-form) + くて / い-adj + くなくて / な-adj + でなくて

examples.japanese: お金がなくて、困っています。
examples.romaji: Okane ga nakute, komatte imasu.
examples.english: I'm in trouble because I don't have money.

examples.japanese: 仕事が忙しくなくて、暇です。
examples.romaji: Shigoto ga isogashiku nakute, hima desu.
examples.english: Work isn't busy and I have free time.

notes: なくて vs ないで: なくて expresses cause/reason or adjective/noun connection; ないで expresses "without doing" or makes a negative request. 食べなくて困る (problematic because not eating) vs 食べないで出かける (go out without eating).

name: 〜なくてもいい
description:
〜なくてもいい expresses that something is not obligatory — 'don't have to,' 'no need to.' The negative て-form (nai-form minus い, plus くて) + もいい: 行かなくてもいい (you don't have to go). It is the negative counterpart to 〜てもいい (you may) and the opposite of 〜なければならない (must).

The pattern can be softened further: 行かなくてもいいです (polite) or 行かなくてもいいよ (casual reassurance). It is widely used to relieve someone of obligation or to give them freedom of choice.

examples.japanese: 今日は来なくてもいいですよ。
examples.romaji: Kyō wa konakute mo ii desu yo.
examples.english: You don't have to come today.

examples.japanese: 全部食べなくてもいいです。
examples.romaji: Zenbu tabenakute mo ii desu.
examples.english: You don't have to eat everything.

notes: 〜なくてもいい = no obligation (you don't have to). 〜てはいけない = prohibition (you must not). 〜なければならない = obligation (you must). Distinguishing these three is fundamental to expressing permission and obligation accurately.

name: 〜なくなる
description:
なくなる combines the negative て-form なく with なる (to become) to mean "to come to not ~", "to no longer ~", or "to run out of". It describes a change of state into a negative condition — something that existed or happened stops existing or happening.

For nouns and adjectives, the equivalent is 〜でなくなる or adj-くなくなる. This pattern expresses gradual or sudden cessation, which is a natural human and situational change.
usage: Verb (ない-stem) + なくなる / Noun + が + なくなる (run out)

examples.japanese: 練習したら、緊張しなくなった。
examples.romaji: Renshuu shitara, kinchou shinaku natta.
examples.english: After practicing, I stopped getting nervous.

examples.japanese: お米がなくなったので、買いに行った。
examples.romaji: Okome ga nakunatta node, kai ni itta.
examples.english: The rice ran out, so I went to buy some.

notes: なくなる (to come to not) vs なくする (to make it not / to lose): なくなる is involuntary or natural change; なくする is deliberate. 財布をなくした (I lost my wallet) vs お金がなくなった (the money ran out/disappeared).

name: 〜なければならない
description:
〜なければならない expresses obligation — 'must,' 'have to.' It attaches to the negative stem (nai-form) of verbs: 行かなければならない (must go). The structure literally means 'if not doing X, it won't do' — creating a double negative that implies necessity.

Variants exist: 〜なくてはならない, 〜なくてはいけない, 〜なければいけない (all roughly equivalent in meaning). In casual speech, these contract heavily: 〜なきゃ, 〜なくちゃ, 〜なくちゃいけない. The more formal the situation, the longer the form used.
usage: Verb (nai stem) + なければならない

examples.japanese: 明日は早く起きなければなりません。
examples.romaji: Ashita wa hayaku okinakereba narimasen.
examples.english: I have to wake up early tomorrow.

examples.japanese: 試験のために勉強しなければならない。
examples.romaji: Shiken no tame ni benkyō shinakereba naranai.
examples.english: I must study for the exam.

notes: Casual contractions are essential to know: 行かなければならない → 行かなきゃ (gotta go), 食べなくてはいけない → 食べなくちゃ. These short forms are extremely common in conversation. The full form sounds stiff in casual contexts.

name: 〜なら
description:
〜なら is a topic-based conditional — 'if that's the case,' 'if it's X we're talking about.' It picks up on something mentioned or implied in context and builds a conditional from it: 日本語を学ぶなら、この本がいい (if you're going to study Japanese, this book is good). It often responds to the other person's statement.

なら attaches to the plain form of verbs/adjectives or directly to nouns (without だ usually): 学生なら (if you're a student / since you're a student). The result clause often contains a recommendation, suggestion, or logical consequence. Unlike たら, なら doesn't require the condition to actually occur.

examples.japanese: 東京に行くなら、スカイツリーは見るべきです。
examples.romaji: Tōkyō ni iku nara, Sukaitsurī wa miru beki desu.
examples.english: If you're going to Tokyo, you should see the Sky Tree.

examples.japanese: 日本語が分からないなら、英語で話しましょう。
examples.romaji: Nihongo ga wakaranai nara, Eigo de hanashimashou.
examples.english: If you don't understand Japanese, let's speak in English.

notes: なら is unique in that the result clause can refer to actions that happen BEFORE the condition is fulfilled: 東京に行くなら、新幹線で行ったほうがいい (if you're going to Tokyo, you should take the Shinkansen — the advice is given before the trip happens). This reversed temporal order is a distinguishing feature of なら.

name: 〜にする
description:
にする has two main meanings. First, it means "to decide on ~" or "to make it ~" — choosing something from options: コーヒーにします (I'll have coffee). Second, with na-adjectives or nouns, it means "to make ~ into ~": 部屋をきれいにする (to make the room clean).

This pattern is highly versatile and appears in many contexts: ordering food, making decisions, changing states. The related pattern にしている means "to make it a habit" or "to keep ~ as".
usage: Noun + にする (decide on) / な-adj + にする (make into)

examples.japanese: 夕食はピザにします。
examples.romaji: Yuushoku wa piza ni shimasu.
examples.english: I'll make it pizza for dinner. (I'll have pizza)

examples.japanese: 机の上をきれいにしてください。
examples.romaji: Tsukue no ue wo kirei ni shite kudasai.
examples.english: Please make the top of the desk clean.

notes: にする (decide on/make into) vs になる (become): にする is an active change by the subject; になる is a natural or spontaneous change. 医者にする (make someone a doctor) vs 医者になる (become a doctor).

name: 〜について
description:
〜について means 'about,' 'regarding,' 'concerning' — it marks the topic of discussion or inquiry. Noun + について: 日本の文化について話した (talked about Japanese culture). The phrase について can stand alone or be followed by の to modify a noun: 日本についての本 (a book about Japan).

について is common in formal and academic contexts: 環境問題について考える (think about environmental issues). It is more formal and specific than the particle に, which can also indicate topic in some contexts.

examples.japanese: 日本の文化について話しましょう。
examples.romaji: Nihon no bunka ni tsuite hanashimashou.
examples.english: Let's talk about Japanese culture.

examples.japanese: この問題についてどう思いますか？
examples.romaji: Kono mondai ni tsuite dō omoimasu ka?
examples.english: What do you think about this issue?

notes: について vs に関して: both mean 'about/regarding,' but に関して (N3) is more formal and limited to serious, academic topics. について is versatile for everyday and formal contexts alike.

name: 〜によって
description:
〜によって has several related meanings: (1) agent in passive sentences — 'by': この小説は夏目漱石によって書かれた (this novel was written by Natsume Soseki); (2) means or method — 'through,' 'by means of': 努力によって成功した (succeeded through effort); (3) cause — 'due to,' 'because of': 地震によって建物が壊れた (the building was destroyed by the earthquake); (4) depending on — によって異なる (varies depending on).

によっては (によっては) adds a conditional nuance — 'depending on the case': 人によっては賛成しない人もいる (depending on the person, some may disagree).

examples.japanese: この小説はシェークスピアによって書かれた。
examples.romaji: Kono shōsetsu wa Shēkusupia ni yotte kakareta.
examples.english: This novel was written by Shakespeare.

examples.japanese: 人によって意見が違います。
examples.romaji: Hito ni yotte iken ga chigaimasu.
examples.english: Opinions differ depending on the person.

notes: によって is often used in formal or written Japanese for passive agents. In casual speech, the passive marker に is more common for agents of passive verbs. The 'depending on' usage is common in both formal and casual contexts.

name: 〜のに
description:
〜のに expresses a concessive contrast — 'even though,' 'despite' — often with a nuance of disappointment, frustration, or unmet expectation. The speaker expected X but got Y: 一生懸命勉強したのに、試験に落ちた (even though I studied so hard, I failed the exam — how unfair).

のに attaches to plain forms. Unlike けど/が (neutral contrast), のに carries an emotional undertone. It is also used in wishful thinking: 行けばよかったのに (if only I had gone). The emotion ranges from mild frustration to regret or criticism.

examples.japanese: 一生懸命勉強したのに、試験に落ちた。
examples.romaji: Isshoukenmei benkyō shita noni, shiken ni ochita.
examples.english: Even though I studied hard, I failed the exam.

examples.japanese: 新しいのに、もう壊れた。
examples.romaji: Atarashii noni, mō kowareta.
examples.english: Even though it's new, it's already broken.

notes: のに (frustrated contrast) vs のでも (even if) vs けど (neutral 'but'). のに always implies an unmet expectation from the speaker's perspective and carries personal emotion. Using のに without that emotional context sounds unnatural.

name: 〜ば
description:
〜ば expresses a hypothetical conditional — 'if (you do X, then Y follows).' It emphasizes the condition: 勉強すれば、合格できる (if you study, you'll pass). Formation: u-verbs drop -u and add -eba; ru-verbs drop -ru and add -reba; い-adj: -i → -kereba; な-adj/noun: -nara.

The ば conditional often implies that the condition is not currently true or is being presented as a hypothetical. It pairs well with advice, suggestions, and logical reasoning. The result clause often cannot be a volitional expression or a direct command.

examples.japanese: 春になれば、桜が咲きます。
examples.romaji: Haru ni nareba, sakura ga sakimasu.
examples.english: If spring comes, the cherry blossoms will bloom.

examples.japanese: もっと安ければ、買うのに。
examples.romaji: Motto yasukereba, kau noni.
examples.english: If it were cheaper, I would buy it.

notes: 〜ば is best for conditions where the result is a natural or logical consequence. Compare conditionals: ば (hypothetical, focuses on condition), たら (sequential or hypothetical, versatile), と (natural/inevitable), なら (topic-based). ば is rarely used when the result is a command or volitional: don't say 行けば行って — use 行ったら instead.

name: 〜はずだ
description:
〜はずだ expresses expectation or assumption based on knowledge, logic, or prior information — 'should be,' 'is supposed to,' 'must be (as far as I know).' It attaches to plain forms: 彼はもう着いているはずだ (he should already have arrived — based on what I know).

はずだ differs from だろう (general conjecture) — はずだ implies there is a logical basis for the expectation. The negative はずがない means 'there's no way that' (strong denial). はずだった expresses an unfulfilled expectation: 来るはずだったのに来なかった (was supposed to come but didn't).

examples.japanese: 彼はもう来ているはずです。
examples.romaji: Kare wa mō kite iru hazu desu.
examples.english: He should already be here.

examples.japanese: この薬を飲めば、よくなるはずだ。
examples.romaji: Kono kusuri o nomeba, yoku naru hazu da.
examples.english: If you take this medicine, you should get better.

notes: はずだ relies on the speaker's knowledge or reasoning. If the actual situation contradicts はずだ, it expresses disappointment or confusion: もう着いているはずなのに… (they should be here by now, but...). Don't use はずだ for pure guesses — use だろう or かもしれない instead.

name: 〜ばよかった
description:
ばよかった expresses regret about something that did not happen — "I should have ~" or "It would have been better if I had ~". It combines the ば-conditional with よかった (it was good / it would be good) in the past tense.

The negative equivalent ばよかった follows a verb in its negative ば-form (〜なければよかった), expressing regret that something DID happen: "I wish I hadn't ~".
usage: Verb (ば-conditional) + よかった / Verb (なければ) + よかった

examples.japanese: もっと早く起きればよかった。
examples.romaji: Motto hayaku okireba yokatta.
examples.english: I should have woken up earlier.

examples.japanese: あんなことを言わなければよかった。
examples.romaji: Anna koto wo iwanakereba yokatta.
examples.english: I wish I hadn't said that.

notes: ばよかった is a fixed expression of regret. It is always in past tense (よかった). The present-tense equivalent ばいい means "should / it would be good if" without the regret nuance. Compare: 早く起きればいい (should get up early) vs 早く起きればよかった (should have gotten up early).

name: 〜ほど
description:
〜ほど expresses degree, approximation, or comparison — 'to the extent that,' 'about,' 'as ... as.' It indicates a degree or amount: 死ぬほど疲れた (so tired I could die — to the extent of dying), 一時間ほど待った (waited about an hour).

ほど also appears in comparative structures: Aほど〜ない (not as ... as A): 彼ほど上手じゃない (not as skilled as him). The more X, the more Y construction uses 〜ば〜ほど: 勉強すればするほど上手になる (the more you study, the better you get).
usage: Noun + ほど / Verb + ほど

examples.japanese: 泣くほど嬉しかった。
examples.romaji: Naku hodo ureshikatta.
examples.english: I was so happy I could cry.

examples.japanese: 彼ほど優しい人はいない。
examples.romaji: Kare hodo yasashii hito wa inai.
examples.english: There is no one as kind as him.

notes: 〜ほど〜ない = not as ... as (A). This is different from 〜より〜ない. 彼ほど背が高くない = (I am) not as tall as him. The 〜ば〜ほど construction (the more... the more) is a set pattern worth memorizing as a unit.

name: 〜やすい・〜にくい
description:
〜やすい and 〜にくい attach to verb stems to express inherent ease or difficulty. やすい means 'easy to do,' 'prone to': 読みやすい本 (an easy-to-read book), 壊れやすい (prone to breaking). にくい means 'hard to do': 食べにくい (hard to eat), 理解しにくい (hard to understand).

Both words conjugate as い-adjectives: やすかった (was easy to do), にくくなる (become harder to do). They describe inherent properties of the subject, not the speaker's personal difficulty. Compare with 〜づらい, which is slightly more subjective and emotional: 言いにくい (hard to say in general) vs 言いづらい (hard for me personally to say).
usage: Verb stem + やすい / にくい

examples.japanese: このペンは書きやすいです。
examples.romaji: Kono pen wa kakiyasui desu.
examples.english: This pen is easy to write with.

examples.japanese: この漢字は覚えにくい。
examples.romaji: Kono kanji wa oboenikui.
examples.english: This kanji is hard to memorize.

notes: やすい/にくい describe objective difficulty inherent to the action or object. づらい (N2) is more subjective — it describes difficulty felt by a specific person, often with emotional discomfort. 言いにくい focuses on the general difficulty; 言いづらい on personal reluctance or discomfort.

name: 〜ようだ
description:
〜ようだ expresses a subjective resemblance or inference — 'it seems/appears that,' 'it looks like,' based on the speaker's personal observation or impression. It conveys that things appear a certain way from the speaker's perspective: 彼は風邪を引いたようだ (it seems he has caught a cold — based on how he looks/acts).

ようだ attaches to plain forms of verbs and adjectives, and to nouns with の (Noun + のようだ). It can also express simile: まるで夢のようだ (it's just like a dream). ように can modify verbs: 夢のように美しい (beautiful like a dream).

examples.japanese: 彼は疲れているようだ。
examples.romaji: Kare wa tsukarete iru yō da.
examples.english: He seems to be tired.

examples.japanese: 外は寒いようです。みんなコートを着ています。
examples.romaji: Soto wa samui yō desu. Minna kōto o kite imasu.
examples.english: It appears to be cold outside. Everyone is wearing coats.

notes: ようだ (subjective inference from personal observation) vs らしい (inference from hearsay/indirect evidence) vs そうだ (appearance from direct visual cues). ようだ is the most subjective — based on the speaker's own perception and reasoning. らしい is more objective — based on external information.

name: 〜らしい
description:
〜らしい expresses hearsay inference or typicality. As inference: 'it seems / apparently' — based on indirect evidence or what one has heard: 田中さんは来ないらしい (it seems Tanaka-san won't come — I've heard). As typicality: 'typical of,' 'like a proper X should be': 男らしい (manly, like a real man), 先生らしい (teacher-like, befitting a teacher).

らしい attaches to the plain form of verbs/adjectives and to nouns directly. It expresses the speaker's inference from secondhand information, not direct perception. It is more objective than ようだ (subjective observation) and less certain than はずだ (expectation from knowledge).

examples.japanese: 田中さんは転職するらしい。
examples.romaji: Tanaka-san wa tenshoku suru rashii.
examples.english: It seems like Tanaka-san is changing jobs.

examples.japanese: あの映画はとても面白いらしいです。
examples.romaji: Ano eiga wa totemo omoshiroi rashii desu.
examples.english: That movie is apparently very interesting.

notes: 三つのらしい: (1) hearsay/inference: 〜らしい after a clause (apparently); (2) typical: Noun + らしい (like a proper/real X); (3) the い-adjective らしい (appearing to be, seeming). Context distinguishes them. Compare: 彼女らしい声 (a voice that seems to be hers) vs 女らしい (feminine, woman-like).

name: 〜間に
description:
〜間に (aida ni) means 'while' or 'during' — something happens within a span of time while another event is ongoing. The key nuance is that the main action (after に) occurs at some point during the duration of the bracketed activity: 彼が寝ている間に、仕事を終えた (while he was sleeping, I finished the work).

With nouns: 休みの間に旅行した (traveled during the break). The difference from ながら: 間に allows different subjects and emphasizes that something happens within a time window, while ながら requires the same subject and emphasizes true simultaneity.
usage: Verb (te iru) + 間に / Noun + の間に

examples.japanese: 子供が寝ている間に、掃除をした。
examples.romaji: Kodomo ga nete iru aida ni, sōji o shita.
examples.english: While the child was sleeping, I cleaned up.

examples.japanese: 夏休みの間に、日本語を上達させたい。
examples.romaji: Natsu yasumi no aida ni, Nihongo o jōtatsu sasetai.
examples.english: I want to improve my Japanese during summer vacation.

notes: 〜ている間に = something happens at a point DURING the duration. 〜ながら = two actions are truly simultaneous by the SAME subject. 先生が話している間に眠くなった (I got sleepy while the teacher was talking — different subjects, occurs within the duration) must use 間に, not ながら.

name: 〜終わる
description:
終わる (おわる) as a compound verb suffix means "to finish doing ~" or "to complete ~". Attached to the masu-stem, it indicates the completion of an action. It describes the natural endpoint of a process.

Note that 終わる is intransitive (no deliberate agent causing the end), while 終える (おえる) is transitive ("to finish/conclude something deliberately"). In compounds, both forms can appear.

examples.japanese: レポートを書き終わりました。
examples.romaji: Repooto wo kakiowari mashita.
examples.english: I finished writing the report.

examples.japanese: 食べ終わったら、お皿を洗ってください。
examples.romaji: Tabeowattara, osara wo aratte kudasai.
examples.english: When you're done eating, please wash the dishes.

notes: Compound verb set: 始める (to start) — 続ける (to continue) — 終わる (to finish). Also contrast: 読み終わる (finish reading a book — natural completion) vs 読み終える (complete the reading — more deliberate/accomplishment-oriented).

name: 〜出す
description:
出す (だす) as a compound verb suffix means "to start ~" or "to begin ~ suddenly". It attaches to the masu-stem of another verb and indicates the abrupt or spontaneous beginning of an action, often one that is difficult to stop once started.

Unlike 始める (to begin, often deliberate and planned), 出す conveys a sudden or uncontrolled onset. It is commonly used with actions like crying (泣き出す), running (走り出す), or laughing (笑い出す).

examples.japanese: 突然、子どもが泣き出した。
examples.romaji: Totsuzen, kodomo ga nakidashita.
examples.english: Suddenly, the child started crying.

examples.japanese: 映画が始まると、彼女は笑い出した。
examples.romaji: Eiga ga hajimaru to, kanojo wa waraidashita.
examples.english: When the movie started, she burst out laughing.

notes: 出す vs 始める: 出す suggests sudden, spontaneous, or involuntary onset; 始める implies a deliberate, planned start. 走り出す (burst into running) vs 走り始める (start running deliberately). Both attach to the masu-stem.

name: 〜場合は
description:
場合は (ばあいは) means "in the case of ~", "in the event that ~", or "if ~". It introduces a hypothetical or specific situation and states what should or does happen under those circumstances. It is more formal than the conditional と or たら.

場合 can be preceded by a noun + の, a verb or adjective in plain form, or a noun phrase. It is frequently used in instructions, manuals, and business communication.
usage: Verb (plain) + 場合は / Noun + の場合は / な-adj + な場合は

examples.japanese: 緊急の場合は、このボタンを押してください。
examples.romaji: Kinkyuu no baai wa, kono botan wo oshite kudasai.
examples.english: In case of an emergency, please press this button.

examples.japanese: 参加できない場合は、事前に連絡してください。
examples.romaji: Sanka dekinai baai wa, jizen ni renraku shite kudasai.
examples.english: If you cannot attend, please contact us in advance.

notes: Compare: 場合は (situation/condition) vs たら (after event, if). 場合は is more formal and often found in written or official instructions. 〜ない場合は (if not ~) is a common variant.

name: 〜通り
description:
〜通り (〜とおり) means "in the same way as / just as / as directed." It indicates an action conforms exactly to something else: 説明書の通りに組み立ててください (please assemble it according to the instructions).

With verbs: 言った通りにやった (I did it exactly as you said). With nouns: の通り — 計画通り (as planned). の can be dropped in compounds: 予定通り (as scheduled), 思い通り (as one wishes).
usage: Verb (dict./た) + 通り(に) / Noun + の通り(に)

examples.japanese: 先生に言われた通りに練習した。
examples.romaji: Sensei ni iwareta toori ni renshuu shita.
examples.english: I practiced exactly as the teacher told me.

examples.japanese: 計画通りに事が進んだ。
examples.romaji: Keikaku doori ni koto ga susunda.
examples.english: Things proceeded as planned.

examples.japanese: ご存知の通り、明日から休みです。
examples.romaji: Gozonji no toori, ashita kara yasumi desu.
examples.english: As you know, we are off from tomorrow.

notes: Note rendaku: in compounds like 計画通り the reading shifts to どおり (keikaku-doori). This voicing happens when 通り follows a noun directly without の.

name: かえって
description:
かえって means "on the contrary / rather / instead" — it introduces an outcome that is the opposite of what was expected. Often the result is worse than anticipated: 薬を飲んだら、かえって具合が悪くなった (after taking the medicine, I actually felt worse).

かえって is an adverb, commonly used in situations where help made things worse or an action produced the reverse of its intended effect.

examples.japanese: 手伝おうとしたが、かえって邪魔になってしまった。
examples.romaji: Tetsudaou to shita ga, kaette jama ni natte shimatta.
examples.english: I tried to help but instead just got in the way.

examples.japanese: 説明しようとしたら、かえって混乱させてしまった。
examples.romaji: Setsumei shiyou to shitara, kaette konran sasete shimatta.
examples.english: When I tried to explain, it just confused them more.

examples.japanese: 早く終わらせようと急いだら、かえって時間がかかった。
examples.romaji: Hayaku owaraseyou to isoidara, kaette jikan ga kakatta.
examples.english: Hurrying to finish quickly actually took longer.

notes: かえって vs むしろ: かえって emphasizes an unexpected reversal; むしろ means "if anything / more accurately" and expresses a preference or more precise description.

name: せっかく
description:
せっかく expresses that something was done "with great effort" or "specially" — and carries a nuance of not wanting to waste that effort. With のに it implies disappointment when effort was wasted: せっかく作ったのに、食べてもらえなかった (I went to all the trouble of making it, but they did not eat it).

In positive contexts, せっかく means "since we have the chance": せっかく来たのだから、ゆっくりしてください (since you have made the trip, please take your time).
usage: せっかく + [effort/opportunity], のに/だから + [result/suggestion]

examples.japanese: せっかく料理したのに、誰も食べなかった。
examples.romaji: Sekkaku ryouri shita noni, daremo tabenakatta.
examples.english: I went to all the trouble of cooking, but nobody ate it.

examples.japanese: せっかく日本に来たのだから、富士山に登りたい。
examples.romaji: Sekkaku nihon ni kita no dakara, fujisan ni noboritai.
examples.english: Since I have gone to the trouble of coming to Japan, I want to climb Mt. Fuji.

examples.japanese: せっかくのチャンスを無駄にしてしまった。
examples.romaji: Sekkaku no chansu wo muda ni shite shimatta.
examples.english: I wasted such a precious opportunity.

notes: せっかく〜のに is one of the most common complaint/regret patterns. せっかくのN means "a special/precious N that should not be wasted."

name: そこで
description:
そこで as a conjunction means "therefore / and so / whereupon" — it connects a situation to a deliberate action taken in response. It indicates what follows is a logical response to the preceding situation.

そこで differs from だから (simple logical "therefore") in that そこで implies the speaker actively decided to act. Commonly used in written narratives and formal speech.

examples.japanese: 資料が足りなかった。そこで図書館に行って調べることにした。
examples.romaji: Shiryou ga tarinakatta. Sokode toshokan ni itte shiraberu koto ni shita.
examples.english: There was not enough material. So I decided to go to the library and research it.

examples.japanese: 風邪をひいた。そこで、早めに医者に行った。
examples.romaji: Kaze wo hiita. Sokode, hayame ni isha ni itta.
examples.english: I caught a cold. So I went to the doctor early.

examples.japanese: 問題が見つかった。そこで担当者に連絡を取った。
examples.romaji: Mondai ga mitsukatta. Sokode tantousha ni renraku wo totta.
examples.english: A problem was found. So I got in contact with the person in charge.

notes: そこで implies human agency — a deliberate decision made in response to a situation. だから is a simple causal connector without this implication.

name: ただ
description:
ただ has two main uses. As an adverb: "just / merely / simply" — ただ見ているだけだ (I am just watching). As a conjunction: "however / but / the only thing is" — introducing a caveat: 賛成です。ただ、条件があります (I agree. However, there is one condition).

As a conjunction, ただ is more measured than でも or けど. It concedes the main point and adds a single calm reservation. Common in formal speech and writing.
usage: ただ + statement / Statement. ただ、+ caveat

examples.japanese: 彼はただ黙って聞いていた。
examples.romaji: Kare wa tada damatte kiite ita.
examples.english: He just silently listened.

examples.japanese: 行ってもいいです。ただ、遅くならないようにしてください。
examples.romaji: Itte mo ii desu. Tada, osoku naranai you ni shite kudasai.
examples.english: You can go. However, please make sure not to be late.

examples.japanese: この仕事、引き受けます。ただ、一つお願いがあります。
examples.romaji: Kono shigoto, hikiukemasu. Tada, hitotsu onegai ga arimasu.
examples.english: I will take on this job. However, I have one request.

notes: ただ as an adjective separately means "free of charge" (ただで食べる) or "ordinary." These are distinct uses.

name: とうとう
description:
とうとう means "finally / at last / in the end" — typically after a long wait or struggle. It can be used for both positive outcomes and negative ones (something dreaded finally came to pass): とうとう試験に落ちてしまった (in the end, I failed after all).

とうとう emphasizes that the result arrived after a long period. When negative, it suggests the speaker saw it coming. Compare with やっと (relief after difficulty) and ついに (neutral and formal).

examples.japanese: 長い冬がとうとう終わった。
examples.romaji: Nagai fuyu ga toutou owatta.
examples.english: The long winter has finally come to an end.

examples.japanese: 彼はとうとう会社を辞めてしまった。
examples.romaji: Kare wa toutou kaisha wo yamete shimatta.
examples.english: He finally quit the company (as expected).

examples.japanese: 待ち続けたが、とうとう彼女は来なかった。
examples.romaji: Machi tsuzuketa ga, toutou kanojo wa konakatta.
examples.english: I kept waiting, but in the end she never came.

notes: とうとう vs やっと vs ついに: とうとう — finally (often inevitable, can be negative); やっと — finally (with relief); ついに — finally (neutral, formal, significant milestones).

name: なかなか
description:
なかなか has two distinct uses. With a positive predicate: "quite / considerably / surprisingly good" — なかなかおいしい (quite delicious — better than expected). With a negative predicate: "not easily / not readily" — なかなか終わらない (it just does not end).

The positive use expresses pleasant surprise. The negative use expresses frustration — despite trying or waiting, the desired result fails to materialize.
usage: なかなか + Positive Predicate / なかなか + Negative Predicate

examples.japanese: この映画はなかなか面白い。
examples.romaji: Kono eiga wa nakanaka omoshiroi.
examples.english: This movie is quite interesting (surprisingly good).

examples.japanese: なかなか眠れなくて困っている。
examples.romaji: Nakanaka nemurenakute komatte iru.
examples.english: I am having trouble — I just cannot get to sleep.

examples.japanese: 彼女はなかなかの腕前だ。
examples.romaji: Kanojo wa nakanaka no udemae da.
examples.english: She is quite skilled (better than you would expect).

notes: Do not confuse with あまり〜ない (not very much), which is about degree rather than difficulty or unexpectedness.

name: 〜がち
description:
がち is a suffix meaning "tend to ~", "apt to ~", or "prone to ~". It indicates a habitual tendency, particularly one that is undesirable or that happens more than expected. It can be attached to verb masu-stems or nouns.

がち describes a pattern of behavior or a recurring state, not a one-time occurrence. The resulting form functions as a な-adjective or noun modifier (がちな人 = a person prone to ~).
usage: Verb (ます-stem) + がち / Noun + がち

examples.japanese: 忙しいとき、食事を抜きがちです。
examples.romaji: Isogashii toki, shokuji wo nukigachi desu.
examples.english: When I'm busy, I tend to skip meals.

examples.japanese: 冬は病気がちになる。
examples.romaji: Fuyu wa byouki gachi ni naru.
examples.english: In winter, I tend to get sick.

notes: がち vs っぽい vs やすい: がち = habitual tendency (usually undesirable); っぽい = seems like/characteristic of (N3); やすい = easy to do, tendency to happen (N4). 忘れがち (tends to forget) vs 忘れやすい (easy to forget, design fault).

name: 〜くせに
description:
〜くせに means 'even though' or 'and yet' — but with a strong critical or contemptuous nuance. It criticizes a discrepancy between what the subject is and what they do: 知らないくせに、偉そうなことを言うな (don't talk big when you don't even know anything). The speaker is clearly dissatisfied or disapproving.

くせに follows plain forms of verbs and adjectives, and nouns with の: 子供のくせに (even though you're just a child). The object of criticism is usually the subject of the sentence. It is more emotional and judgmental than のに (which is a neutral or mildly frustrated contrast).

examples.japanese: 知っているくせに、教えてくれない。
examples.romaji: Shitte iru kuse ni, oshiete kurenai.
examples.english: Even though you know, you won't tell me.

examples.japanese: 子供のくせに、生意気なことを言う。
examples.romaji: Kodomo no kuse ni, namaiki na koto wo iu.
examples.english: Even though he's just a child, he says impudent things.

notes: 〜くせに (critical, contemptuous) vs 〜のに (frustrated/disappointed but neutral). Use くせに only when expressing clear criticism or looking down on someone. Using it carelessly can sound very rude. It is inappropriate in formal writing or toward superiors.

name: 〜ことにする
description:
〜ことにする expresses a deliberate personal decision — 'I have decided to,' 'I will make it a point to.' The speaker takes full ownership of the choice: 毎朝ジョギングすることにした (I've decided to jog every morning). It contrasts with ことになる, where the decision comes from external circumstances.

ことにしている (habitual form) expresses a personal rule or habit one has made for oneself: 毎日日本語を勉強することにしている (I make it a habit to study Japanese every day). The negative ことにした = 'I've decided not to': 食べないことにした (I've decided not to eat it).

examples.japanese: 健康のために、毎朝走ることにした。
examples.romaji: Kenkō no tame ni, maiasa hashiru koto ni shita.
examples.english: I decided to run every morning for my health.

examples.japanese: もう甘いものは食べないことにします。
examples.romaji: Mō amai mono wa tabenai koto ni shimasu.
examples.english: I've decided not to eat sweets anymore.

notes: 〜ことにする = my own deliberate decision. 〜ことになる = external decision that came about. The distinction matters for expressing agency. If you want to show you chose something, use ことにする. If you want to show circumstances led to an outcome (distancing yourself), use ことになる.

name: 〜ことになる
description:
〜ことになる expresses that a situation has been decided or will come about due to external circumstances — 'it has been decided that,' 'it turns out that.' The speaker is often not the decision-maker: 来月、転勤することになった (it has been decided that I'll transfer next month — the company decided). It implies the outcome arose from outside forces.

ことになっている expresses a rule or established custom: 会議は毎週月曜日にあることになっている (meetings are scheduled every Monday — that's the established arrangement). The plain form + ことになる vs ことにする: the former is external decision; the latter is the speaker's own decision.

examples.japanese: 来月、大阪に転勤することになった。
examples.romaji: Raigetsu, Ōsaka ni tenkin suru koto ni natta.
examples.english: It has been decided that I'll be transferred to Osaka next month.

examples.japanese: 会議は木曜日に行うことになっています。
examples.romaji: Kaigi wa mokuyōbi ni okonau koto ni natte imasu.
examples.english: It has been decided that the meeting will be held on Thursday.

notes: 〜ことになる (external decision — it came to be decided) vs 〜ことにする (personal decision — I have decided). Use ことになる to distance yourself from the decision; use ことにする to take ownership. 転勤することになった = I'm being transferred (company decided). 転勤することにした = I decided to transfer.

name: 〜さえ
description:
〜さえ means 'even' — it emphasizes an extreme or unexpected case, often implying that if even this much holds true, the rest certainly does: 子供でさえ知っている (even children know it — so surely adults do). It intensifies by presenting an extreme example.

さえ replaces or combines with particles: に+さえ → にさえ, で+さえ → でさえ. In the pattern 〜さえ〜ば (N3), さえ marks the single sufficient condition: これさえあれば十分だ (as long as I have this, it's enough). Compare with も (also/even) which is less emphatic.
usage: Noun + さえ / Verb stem + さえ

examples.japanese: 疲れすぎて、食事さえ忘れた。
examples.romaji: Tsukaresugite, shokuji sae wasureta.
examples.english: I was so tired that I even forgot to eat.

examples.japanese: 子供さえ知っているような常識だ。
examples.romaji: Kodomo sae shitte iru yō na jōshiki da.
examples.english: It's common knowledge that even children know.

notes: さえ vs でさえ vs すら: all mean 'even' but with slightly different registers. さえ is standard; でさえ adds emphasis to the preceding noun; すら (N1) is more literary/formal. すら is the strongest: 子供すら知っている sounds more dramatic than 子供でさえ知っている.

name: 〜さえ〜ば
description:
〜さえ〜ば expresses a single sufficient condition — 'as long as,' 'if only.' It narrows down all requirements to one key thing: お金さえあれば、何でも買える (as long as I have money, I can buy anything). The implication is that this one condition is the only thing needed.

The structure pairs さえ with the ば conditional: Noun + さえ + Verb (ba-form). It can also take adjective or noun + であれば: 元気さえあれば (as long as you have energy). The nuance is optimistic — 'just this one thing is enough.'

examples.japanese: お金さえあれば、旅行に行けるのに。
examples.romaji: Okane sae areba, ryokō ni ikeru no ni.
examples.english: If only I had money, I could go traveling.

examples.japanese: 毎日練習さえすれば、必ず上手になれる。
examples.romaji: Mainichi renshū sae sureba, kanarazu jōzu ni nareru.
examples.english: As long as you practice every day, you will definitely get better.

notes: 〜さえ〜ば (single sufficient condition: just this much is enough) vs 〜しか〜ない (only this, nothing more — implies insufficiency). さえ〜ば is forward-looking and often positive; しか〜ない is restrictive and often implies a lack. Both highlight a single item but with different emotional tones.

name: 〜だけでなく
description:
〜だけでなく(て) means 'not only... but also' — it extends a quality or action beyond the first element to an additional one: 日本語だけでなく、中国語も話せる (not only Japanese, but also Chinese). The additional element is often introduced with も.

The structure emphasizes that the scope is broader than might be expected. Formal variants include 〜のみならず (N2) and 〜ばかりでなく (N2). The て-form (だけでなくて) is slightly more casual; だけでなく alone is neutral to formal.
usage: Noun / Verb (dict.) + だけでなく(て) + も

examples.japanese: 彼は英語だけでなく、中国語も話せる。
examples.romaji: Kare wa eigo dake de naku, chūgokugo mo hanaseru.
examples.english: He can speak not only English but also Chinese.

examples.japanese: この問題は日本だけでなく、世界中に影響を与えている。
examples.romaji: Kono mondai wa Nihon dake de naku, sekaijū ni eikyō wo ataete iru.
examples.english: This problem affects not only Japan but the entire world.

notes: 〜だけでなく〜も = not only A but also B. Both elements should be from the same category or parallel in structure. The も after the second element is often required but can be omitted in some patterns. This structure is commonly paired with さらに (furthermore) in formal writing.

name: 〜たびに
description:
たびに means "every time ~", "whenever ~", or "each time ~". It indicates that the main clause action happens consistently each time the preceding event or condition occurs. It expresses a reliable, repeatable connection between two events.

たびに follows dictionary-form verbs or nouns + の. The result in the main clause is usually a recurring feeling, habit, or outcome. It implies some degree of regularity.
usage: Verb (dictionary form) + たびに / Noun + の + たびに

examples.japanese: 日本に来るたびに、新しい発見がある。
examples.romaji: Nihon ni kuru tabi ni, atarashii hakken ga aru.
examples.english: Every time I come to Japan, I make new discoveries.

examples.japanese: 失敗のたびに、また頑張ろうと思う。
examples.romaji: Shippai no tabi ni, mata ganbarou to omou.
examples.english: Every time I fail, I think I'll try again.

notes: たびに vs ごとに: たびに emphasizes the repeated occasion ("each time X happens"); ごとに is more regularly systematic ("every X"). 電車に乗るたびに (every time I ride the train) vs 一時間ごとに (every hour). ごとに is more mechanical; たびに more event-driven.

name: 〜だらけ
description:
だらけ is a suffix attached to nouns meaning "full of ~", "covered with ~", or "riddled with ~". It conveys a sense that something is undesirably or excessively filled with the noun. It always carries a negative nuance — used for things like mistakes, mud, or problems that you would rather not have.

だらけ follows nouns directly without any particle. It cannot be used for positive things (you would not say 花だらけ = "full of flowers" unless complaining). The resulting phrase functions like a な-adjective or noun modifier.

examples.japanese: このレポートは間違いだらけだ。
examples.romaji: Kono repooto wa machigai darake da.
examples.english: This report is full of mistakes.

examples.japanese: 公園で遊んで、子どもが泥だらけになった。
examples.romaji: Kouen de asonde, kodomo ga doro darake ni natta.
examples.english: After playing in the park, the child became covered in mud.

notes: だらけ always implies an undesirable excess. Compare: まみれ (smeared with, physical coating) vs だらけ (riddled with, general excess). 血まみれ (covered in blood — smeared) vs 間違いだらけ (full of mistakes). Also compare: ずくめ (all ~, positive or neutral) vs だらけ (negative excess).

name: 〜つつ
description:
つつ has two uses. (1) "While ~" — indicating two actions happening simultaneously, similar to ながら but more formal/literary. (2) "Even though ~" or "despite ~" — often written つつも — expressing contrast between awareness/feeling and action (knowing something is wrong, yet still doing it).

つつ is formal and appears in written language, literary texts, and formal speeches. In everyday conversation, ながら (simultaneous) or のに/けど (contrast) are more natural.
usage: Verb (ます-stem) + つつ / Verb (ます-stem) + つつも

examples.japanese: 音楽を聴きつつ、勉強する。
examples.romaji: Ongaku wo kikitsutsu, benkyou suru.
examples.english: I study while listening to music.

examples.japanese: 悪いと知りつつも、つい食べ過ぎてしまう。
examples.romaji: Warui to shiritsutsu mo, tsui tabesugi te shimau.
examples.english: Even knowing it's bad for me, I end up overeating.

notes: つつ vs ながら: both mean "while doing". ながら is casual and common; つつ is formal/literary. つつも (despite) is an important nuance — it shows the speaker acknowledges something is wrong yet continues anyway. Common in confessional or introspective statements.

name: 〜っぱなし
description:
〜っぱなし attaches to the ます-stem and means "leaving something in a state" or "continually doing something." It often implies neglect: ドアを開けっぱなしにする (to leave the door open, neglecting to close it).

It also describes a continuous action that goes on too long: 立ちっぱなしで疲れた (tired from standing the whole time). In both uses, a state began and was never properly ended.

examples.japanese: 蛇口を開けっぱなしにしないでください。
examples.romaji: Jaguchi wo akeppanashi ni shinaide kudasai.
examples.english: Please do not leave the faucet running.

examples.japanese: 彼はドアを開けっぱなしにして出て行った。
examples.romaji: Kare wa doa wo akeppanashi ni shite dete itta.
examples.english: He went out leaving the door wide open.

examples.japanese: 今日は歩きっぱなしで足が痛い。
examples.romaji: Kyou wa arukippanashi de ashi ga itai.
examples.english: I have been walking non-stop today and my feet hurt.

notes: Common combinations: 開けっぱなし (left open), つけっぱなし (left on), 出しっぱなし (left out), 立ちっぱなし (standing the whole time). Often implies neglect.

name: 〜っぽい
description:
〜っぽい attaches to nouns, verb ます-stems, or adjective stems and means "-ish / tending to / seeming like": 子供っぽい (childish), 忘れっぽい (tends to forget), 白っぽい (whitish).

っぽい typically has a casual, slightly negative nuance — something has characteristics of X but is not quite X, or describes an undesirable habitual tendency.
usage: Noun / Verb (ます-stem) / Adjective stem + っぽい

examples.japanese: そんな子供っぽいことを言わないで。
examples.romaji: Sonna kodomo-ppoi koto wo iwanaide.
examples.english: Do not say such childish things.

examples.japanese: 彼女は飽きっぽい性格だ。
examples.romaji: Kanojo wa akippoi seikaku da.
examples.english: She has a personality that tends to get bored easily.

examples.japanese: この服、安っぽく見えない？
examples.romaji: Kono fuku, yasuppoku mienai?
examples.english: Does this outfit look cheap?

notes: Common words: 飽きっぽい (easily bored), 忘れっぽい (forgetful), 怒りっぽい (quick to anger), 子供っぽい (childish), 安っぽい (cheap-looking). Conjugates like a regular い-adjective.

name: 〜てしかたがない
description:
〜てしかたがない (also written 〜て仕方がない) expresses an overwhelming, uncontrollable feeling or urge — 'can't help but feel,' 'unbearably,' 'I can't stop doing/feeling.' The emotion or sensation is so strong that the speaker is at its mercy: 眠くてしかたがない (I'm unbearably sleepy).

It attaches to the て-form of verbs or adjectives: 気になってしかたがない (I can't stop being curious), 悲しくてしかたがない (unbearably sad). Synonym: てたまらない (same meaning, slightly stronger). Both express that the feeling exceeds what the speaker can control.
usage: Verb (te) / Adj (te) + しかたがない

examples.japanese: 試験の結果が気になってしかたがない。
examples.romaji: Shiken no kekka ga ki ni natte shikata ga nai.
examples.english: I can't help worrying about the exam results.

examples.japanese: 昨日から眠くてしかたがない。
examples.romaji: Kinō kara nemukute shikata ga nai.
examples.english: I've been unbearably sleepy since yesterday.

notes: 〜てしかたがない vs 〜てたまらない: essentially synonymous. たまらない is arguably slightly stronger or more visceral. Both are used for physical sensations and emotions that are overwhelming. Don't use them for external situations — use them for internal states the speaker is experiencing.

name: 〜てたまらない
description:
〜てたまらない expresses an overwhelming, irresistible feeling or desire — 'can't stand it,' 'unbearably,' 'I simply can't take it.' It expresses that the feeling exceeds the speaker's capacity to control: 彼女に会いたくてたまらない (I desperately want to see her — I can't take it anymore).

たまらない (literally: 'there's no enduring it') attaches to the て-form of adjectives and verbs. It is slightly more visceral and passionate than しかたがない. It can be used positively (overwhelmingly wonderful) or negatively (overwhelmingly unpleasant): 美味しくてたまらない (so delicious I can't stand it).

examples.japanese: 辛いものを食べたくてたまらない。
examples.romaji: Karai mono wo tabetakute tamaranai.
examples.english: I can't stand how much I want to eat spicy food.

examples.japanese: その映画のことが頭から離れなくてたまらない。
examples.romaji: Sono eiga no koto ga atama kara hanarenakute tamaranai.
examples.english: I can't stop thinking about that movie.

notes: 〜てたまらない vs 〜てしかたがない: both express being overwhelmed by a feeling. たまらない is slightly more emotionally intense. Both are internal states; neither applies to situations or events themselves. These cannot be used in formal writing — they are conversational.

name: 〜ということ
description:
ということ (often contracted to ってこと in casual speech) nominalizes a clause and means "the fact that ~", "that ~", "meaning that ~". It is used to refer to a fact, idea, or situation as a noun phrase — often to explain, emphasize, or confirm what has been said.

ということは leads to a conclusion: "that means ~" / "so that means ~". It is extremely common in spoken Japanese when confirming understanding or drawing inferences from information.
usage: Verb/い-adj (plain) + ということ / な-adj + だということ / Noun + だということ

examples.japanese: 合格したということは、努力が実ったということだ。
examples.romaji: Goukaku shita to iu koto wa, doryoku ga minotta to iu koto da.
examples.english: The fact that I passed means my efforts paid off.

examples.japanese: 電車が止まっているということは、事故があったのかもしれない。
examples.romaji: Densha ga tomatte iru to iu koto wa, jiko ga atta no kamo shirenai.
examples.english: The fact that the train has stopped may mean there was an accident.

notes: ということ can wrap entire sentences as noun clauses for embedding. ということは (at the start of a sentence) = "that means" / "which means". Casual: ってこと (in speech). Compare: のこと (about ~) vs ということ (the fact that ~).

name: 〜というのは
description:
〜というのは introduces an explanation or definition — 'the thing called X is,' 'what I mean by X is.' It takes a term or concept and defines or explains it: 「侘び寂び」というのは、日本の美的概念だ (what is called 'wabi-sabi' is a Japanese aesthetic concept).

というのは is a more explicit version of とは (which also introduces definitions). It is commonly used when explaining unfamiliar terms, jargon, or concepts to a listener. Contracted to というのは → つまり/要するに in summaries.

examples.japanese: 「おせち」というのは、お正月に食べる伝統的な料理のことだ。
examples.romaji: "Osechi" to iu no wa, oshōgatsu ni taberu dentōteki na ryōri no koto da.
examples.english: "Osechi" refers to traditional dishes eaten at New Year's.

examples.japanese: 友達というのは、困った時に助けてくれる人だ。
examples.romaji: Tomodachi to iu no wa, komatta toki ni tasukete kureru hito da.
examples.english: A friend is someone who helps you when you are in trouble.

notes: 〜というのは (explains/defines a term) can be shortened to simply 〜とは: 愛とは何か (what is love?). というのは is more conversational and elaborate; とは is more formal and terse. Both introduce definitions or conceptual explanations.

name: 〜というより
description:
というより means "rather than saying ~", "more than ~", or "it's not so much ~ as it is ~". It corrects or refines a description, suggesting that the second description is more accurate or fitting than the first one given.

It is used when the speaker wants to nuance or replace a previous statement with something they consider more precise. Often the speaker is correcting their own or another's word choice.
usage: Noun/Verb/Adj (plain) + というより + (more accurate description)

examples.japanese: 彼は怒っているというより、悲しんでいる。
examples.romaji: Kare wa okotte iru to iu yori, kanashinde iru.
examples.english: Rather than being angry, he is sad.

examples.japanese: これは趣味というより、もはや仕事だ。
examples.romaji: Kore wa shumi to iu yori, mohaya shigoto da.
examples.english: This is no longer a hobby — it's basically a job.

notes: というより connects two descriptions and subordinates the first to the second. Compare: 〜より (simple comparison: A more than B) vs というより (correction: not so much A, but rather B). The first element is the rejected or insufficient description.

name: 〜といっても
description:
といっても means "even though I say ~", "although ~ may be said", or "even if we call it ~". It acknowledges that a statement sounds a certain way but immediately qualifies or limits it, preventing misunderstanding or exaggeration.

The speaker uses それ that might create an expectation, then says といっても to qualify it: "I say I'm studying, but it's only 30 minutes a day." It manages listener expectations.

examples.japanese: 毎日運動しているといっても、10分程度です。
examples.romaji: Mainichi undou shite iru to itte mo, juppun teido desu.
examples.english: Even though I say I exercise every day, it's only about 10 minutes.

examples.japanese: 日本語が話せるといっても、まだ初心者です。
examples.romaji: Nihongo ga hanaseru to itte mo, mada shoshinsha desu.
examples.english: Even though I can speak Japanese, I'm still a beginner.

notes: といっても is a softening/qualification tool. It prevents the listener from forming an inflated impression. Common structure: [Possibly impressive claim] + といっても + [limiting reality]. Contrast with 〜とはいえ (N2) which has a similar structure but more formal register.

name: 〜ところ
description:
〜ところ expresses a specific temporal moment in relation to an action. Three key patterns: (1) Dict. form + ところ = 'just about to': 今から行くところだ (I'm just about to go); (2) て-form + いるところ = 'in the middle of': 今食べているところだ (I'm in the middle of eating right now); (3) た-form + ところ = 'just finished': 今食べたところだ (I just finished eating).

ところ literally means 'place/point,' and these patterns indicate the point in time relative to the action. Each variant answers a different question about what stage the action is at.
usage: Verb (dict.) + ところ / (ta) + ところだった

examples.japanese: 今から出かけるところです。
examples.romaji: Ima kara dekakeru tokoro desu.
examples.english: I'm just about to go out now.

examples.japanese: 危ないところだった！
examples.romaji: Abunai tokoro datta!
examples.english: That was a close call!

notes: The three ところ patterns are distinguished by verb form: dictionary (about to) → ている (in progress) → た (just finished). This maps to before → during → after. Compare 〜たばかり (just did — similar to たところだ, but ばかり has a broader time window and doesn't require immediacy).

name: 〜ところが
description:
ところが is a conjunction meaning "however", "but", or "and yet". It introduces an unexpected or surprising contrast — the result that follows is contrary to what one would naturally expect from the preceding clause. Unlike でも or が, ところが places strong emphasis on the unexpected nature of the contrast.

ところが typically appears at the beginning of the second sentence or clause, and the contrast it introduces is often significant or emotionally impactful.

examples.japanese: 試験は難しいと思っていた。ところが、簡単だった。
examples.romaji: Shiken wa muzukashii to omotte ita. Tokoro ga, kantan datta.
examples.english: I thought the exam would be difficult. However, it was easy.

examples.japanese: 傘を持ってきた。ところが、一度も雨が降らなかった。
examples.romaji: Kasa wo motte kita. Tokoro ga, ichido mo ame ga furanakatta.
examples.english: I brought an umbrella. But it didn't rain even once.

notes: ところが (unexpected reversal) vs でも (simple contrast) vs けれども (mild contrast). ところが carries the strongest sense of "surprisingly, the opposite happened". It cannot be used when the contrast is mild or expected; only for genuinely surprising turns.

name: 〜となると
description:
となると means "when it comes to ~", "if that's the case", or "given that ~". It introduces a topic or condition and indicates what naturally follows — often a consequence, implication, or inference that flows logically from the situation stated before.

となると signals that the speaker is reasoning from a premise to a conclusion. It is often used to discuss implications or necessary next steps.
usage: Noun + となると / Verb (plain) + となると

examples.japanese: 来週が締め切りとなると、急がなければならない。
examples.romaji: Raishu ga shimekiri to naru to, isoganakere ba naranai.
examples.english: If next week is the deadline, then we have to hurry.

examples.japanese: 彼が来ないとなると、計画を変える必要がある。
examples.romaji: Kare ga konai to naru to, keikaku wo kaeru hitsuyou ga aru.
examples.english: If he's not coming, we need to change our plans.

notes: となると is used for logical inference from a situation, often triggering discussion of what must follow. Compare: となれば (more hypothetical: if it becomes ~) and となると (more concrete: given that it's the case that ~).

name: 〜ないことはない
description:
〜ないことはない (also 〜ないこともない) is a double negative meaning "it is not impossible / I would not say not." It cautiously admits something is possible without fully committing: 行けないことはないが、あまり気が進まない (it is not that I cannot go, but I am not feeling up to it).

This gives a guarded affirmative — the speaker concedes a possibility while hedging. Often followed by a qualification.
usage: Verb (ない-form) + ことはない / こともない

examples.japanese: できないことはないが、かなり難しい。
examples.romaji: Dekinai koto wa nai ga, kanari muzukashii.
examples.english: It is not impossible, but it is quite difficult.

examples.japanese: 理解できないこともないが、賛成はしかねる。
examples.romaji: Rikai dekinai koto mo nai ga, sansei wa shikaneru.
examples.english: I cannot say I do not understand, but I cannot agree either.

examples.japanese: 一人で行けないことはないけど、誰かと一緒のほうが安心だ。
examples.romaji: Hitori de ikenai koto wa nai kedo, dareka to issho no hou ga anshin da.
examples.english: It is not that I cannot go alone, but I would feel safer with someone.

notes: ことはない is slightly more emphatic than こともない. Compare with 〜わけではない (it is not the case that) which negates a stronger assumption.

name: 〜ながらも
description:
〜ながらも means 'although,' 'even while,' 'despite' — it expresses a contradiction or unexpected contrast between two simultaneous states. Unlike ながら alone (simply 'while doing'), ながらも adds a concessive nuance: 知りながらも、黙っていた (even though knowing, I stayed silent — the two coexist in tension).

ながらも attaches to verb stems and adjective stems, or follows the plain form after 〜でありながらも (for nouns/な-adj). It is more literary and formal than のに or けれども, and often appears in written Japanese.
usage: Verb stem / Adj stem + ながらも

examples.japanese: 小さいながらも、その犬は勇敢だった。
examples.romaji: Chiisai nagara mo, sono inu wa yūkan datta.
examples.english: Although small, that dog was brave.

examples.japanese: 失敗しながらも、彼はあきらめなかった。
examples.romaji: Shippai shinagara mo, kare wa akiramenakatta.
examples.english: Even while failing, he did not give up.

notes: 〜ながら (while doing — simultaneous actions, same subject) vs 〜ながらも (although/even while — concessive, contradiction). ながらも implies the two things should not logically coexist but do. Compare: 音楽を聴きながら勉強する (study while listening — simultaneous) vs 忙しいながらも手伝った (helped even though busy — concessive).

name: 〜において
description:
〜において means 'in,' 'at,' 'in the context of' — a formal equivalent of で for location or context. It marks the setting, sphere, or condition in which something occurs: 世界において (in the world), スポーツにおける成功 (success in sports). It is common in academic, political, and formal written language.

The attributive form における modifies nouns: 日本における問題 (problems in Japan). において emphasizes that the preceding noun defines the domain or context of the statement, not just the physical place.
usage: Noun + において / における + Noun

examples.japanese: 現代社会において、インターネットは欠かせない。
examples.romaji: Gendai shakai ni oite, intānetto wa kakasenai.
examples.english: In modern society, the internet is indispensable.

examples.japanese: 式典は大ホールにおいて行われた。
examples.romaji: Shikiten wa ō hōru ni oite okonawareta.
examples.english: The ceremony was held in the grand hall.

notes: 〜において is a formal/written equivalent of で (location of action). In casual speech, always use で: 学校で (at school). Use において in essays, reports, official documents, and formal presentations. における is the noun-modifying version: 現代における課題 (challenges in modern times).

name: 〜につれて
description:
につれて means "as ~" or "in proportion to ~". It expresses that as one thing changes or progresses, another thing changes correspondingly. Both changes happen simultaneously and in the same direction (both increase, or one increases as the other decreases).

The clause before につれて is typically a verb (dictionary form) or noun expressing a progressive change. The main clause shows the resulting change.
usage: Verb (dictionary) + につれて / Noun + につれて

examples.japanese: 年をとるにつれて、記憶力が落ちてきた。
examples.romaji: Toshi wo toru ni tsurete, kiokuryoku ga ochite kita.
examples.english: As I grew older, my memory started to decline.

examples.japanese: 技術の発展につれて、生活が便利になった。
examples.romaji: Gijutsu no hatten ni tsurete, seikatsu ga benri ni natta.
examples.english: As technology developed, life became more convenient.

notes: につれて vs にしたがって: both mean "as ~ progresses". につれて emphasizes parallel/proportional change; にしたがって can also mean "following instructions". Both typically require a progressive/ongoing change, not a one-time event.

name: 〜にとって
description:
〜にとって means 'for,' 'to,' 'from the perspective of' — it marks the entity for whom something holds true or is meaningful: 私にとって、日本語は難しい (Japanese is difficult for me / from my perspective). It indicates whose point of view or whose benefit is being discussed.

にとって attaches to nouns and pronouns. It cannot be used with actions that the marked person directly performs — it marks who is affected by or views a situation: 子供にとって大切なこと (things that are important for children). It is similar to 〜にとっては when emphasizing the specific viewpoint.

examples.japanese: この仕事は私にとって大切な経験だ。
examples.romaji: Kono shigoto wa watashi ni totte taisetsu na keiken da.
examples.english: This job is an important experience for me.

examples.japanese: 子供にとって、親の愛情は不可欠だ。
examples.romaji: Kodomo ni totte, oya no aijō wa fukaketsu da.
examples.english: Parental love is indispensable for children.

notes: 〜にとって (subjective perspective/benefit) vs 〜のために (purpose/objective benefit). にとって is about whose standpoint matters; のために is about who benefits from an action. 子供にとって大切 (important from the child's perspective) vs 子供のために勉強する (study for the sake of the child).

name: 〜に違いない
description:
〜に違いない expresses confident deductive certainty — 'must be,' 'there's no doubt that,' 'I'm certain that.' The speaker reasons from evidence and concludes with conviction: 彼が犯人に違いない (he must be the culprit — I'm certain). It is stronger than でしょう or かもしれない.

に違いない attaches to the plain form of verbs/adjectives and to nouns (Noun + に違いない). The certainty is based on the speaker's reasoning, not on reported information. It is one step below absolute certainty — the speaker is making a strong deduction, not a statement of fact.

examples.japanese: 電気がついているから、誰かいるに違いない。
examples.romaji: Denki ga tsuite iru kara, dareka iru ni chigai nai.
examples.english: The light is on, so someone must be there.

examples.japanese: こんなに練習したのだから、合格するに違いない。
examples.romaji: Konna ni renshū shita no dakara, gōkaku suru ni chigai nai.
examples.english: I've practiced this much, so I'm certain to pass.

notes: Certainty scale: 〜に相違ない (N1, very formal) ≈ 〜に違いない (N3, certain) > 〜はずだ (N4, expected) > 〜でしょう (N4, probably) > 〜かもしれない (N4, might). Use に違いない when your reasoning strongly points to one conclusion.

name: 〜に関して
description:
〜に関して means 'regarding,' 'concerning,' 'about' — it is a slightly more formal and specific alternative to について. It is commonly used in written Japanese, formal speech, business, and academic contexts: 環境問題に関して話し合った (we discussed regarding environmental issues).

に関して attaches to nouns. The attributive form に関する modifies nouns: 環境に関する問題 (problems concerning the environment). Unlike について, に関して is rarely used for casual topics — it implies a formal or serious matter.
usage: Noun + に関して / に関する + Noun

examples.japanese: この問題に関して、詳しく説明します。
examples.romaji: Kono mondai ni kanshite, kuwashiku setsumei shimasu.
examples.english: I will explain this issue in detail.

examples.japanese: 環境問題に関する本を読んだ。
examples.romaji: Kankyō mondai ni kansuru hon wo yonda.
examples.english: I read a book concerning environmental issues.

notes: に関して vs について: both mean 'about/regarding,' but に関して is more formal, specific, and limited to serious or important matters. In everyday conversation, について is more natural. In reports, letters, and formal meetings, に関して is preferred.

name: 〜に限って
description:
〜に限って means "only ~", "just ~" or more specifically carries an ironic nuance: "of all things/people, precisely ~". It is used to express that something happens specifically in the case of the noun mentioned — often in surprising, ironic, or unfortunate circumstances.

A common use is to express that the case you hoped would be different is exactly the one where the unwanted thing happens: 大事な日に限って (just when it's an important day, of all days).

examples.japanese: 大事な日に限って、電車が遅れる。
examples.romaji: Daiji na hi ni kagitte, densha ga okureru.
examples.english: Just when it's an important day, the train runs late.

examples.japanese: うちの子に限って、そんなことはしない。
examples.romaji: Uchi no ko ni kagitte, sonna koto wa shinai.
examples.english: My child would never do such a thing. (Not my child!)

notes: に限って often implies irony (Murphy's law type situations) or the speaker's conviction that a particular case is special/exempt. Compare: に限らず (not limited to); に限り (limited to, only for — without the irony).

name: 〜に限らず
description:
に限らず means "not limited to ~", "not only ~", or "regardless of ~". It indicates that what follows is true beyond the specific noun mentioned — extending the scope to a broader range. It is the opposite of に限り (limited to).

に限らず commonly appears with specific groups, locations, or conditions as the noun, expanding the statement to include more than just that one case.

examples.japanese: 日本に限らず、世界中でこの問題が起きている。
examples.romaji: Nihon ni kagirazu, sekaijuu de kono mondai ga okite iru.
examples.english: Not limited to Japan, this problem is occurring throughout the world.

examples.japanese: 子どもに限らず、大人もこのゲームを楽しんでいる。
examples.romaji: Kodomo ni kagirazu, otona mo kono geemu wo tanoshinde iru.
examples.english: Not just children, but adults also enjoy this game.

notes: に限らず vs だけでなく: both mean "not only ~". に限らず is slightly more formal; だけでなく is more conversational. に限らず typically expands from a specific group outward, while だけでなく can add items more freely.

name: 〜に従って
description:
に従って has two related meanings. (1) "In accordance with ~", "following ~" — acting in compliance with rules, instructions, or orders. (2) "As ~ progresses" / "proportionally with ~" — indicating that as one thing changes, another changes accordingly, like 〜につれて.

When expressing compliance, the noun before に従って is a rule, order, or guide. When expressing proportional change, it is more like "as time passes" or "as practice increases".
usage: Noun + に従って / Verb (dictionary) + に従って

examples.japanese: 規則に従って行動してください。
examples.romaji: Kisoku ni shitagatte koudou shite kudasai.
examples.english: Please act in accordance with the rules.

examples.japanese: 練習するに従って、上手になっていった。
examples.romaji: Renshuu suru ni shitagatte, jouzu ni natte itta.
examples.english: As I practiced, I kept getting better.

notes: に従って (following/proportional) vs に基づいて (based on) vs に沿って (along with/in line with). に従って implies following a directive or changing proportionally; に基づいて means grounded in a basis or foundation.

name: 〜に対して
description:
〜に対して has three related meanings: (1) directed toward/at: 彼に対して怒りを感じた (I felt anger directed at him); (2) in response to, regarding: 質問に対して答える (answer the question); (3) in contrast to — comparing two things: A は良い成績だったのに対して、B は悪かった (A got good grades, whereas B got bad grades).

に対して modifies the following verb or is used in the attributive form に対する before nouns: 彼への/彼に対する態度 (attitude toward him). The contrast usage is common in formal writing and essays.
usage: Noun + に対して / に対する + Noun

examples.japanese: 先生に対して失礼な態度をとってはいけない。
examples.romaji: Sensei ni taishite shitsurei na taido wo totte wa ikenai.
examples.english: You must not behave rudely towards your teacher.

examples.japanese: 彼は批判に対して冷静に答えた。
examples.romaji: Kare wa hihan ni taishite reisei ni kotaeta.
examples.english: He responded calmly to the criticism.

notes: に対して (directed at / in contrast to) vs について (concerning/about). に対して implies a response, opposition, or contrast; について is more neutral and just indicates the topic. 問題に対して対応する (deal with the problem) vs 問題について話す (talk about the problem).

name: 〜に比べて
description:
〜に比べて means 'compared to,' 'in comparison with' — it sets up a comparison between two entities: 去年に比べて、今年は暖かい (compared to last year, this year is warm). The noun preceding に比べて is the reference point for comparison.

に比べて can modify verbs, adjectives, or entire clauses. The attributive form に比べた is used before nouns: 去年に比べた変化 (changes compared to last year). Related expression: に比べ (slightly more formal, without て).

examples.japanese: 去年に比べて、今年は暖かい。
examples.romaji: Kyonen ni kurabete, kotoshi wa atatakai.
examples.english: Compared to last year, this year is warm.

examples.japanese: 都会に比べて、田舎は物価が安い。
examples.romaji: Tokai ni kurabete, inaka wa bukka ga yasui.
examples.english: Compared to the city, the countryside has lower prices.

notes: 〜に比べて (explicit comparison with a reference point) vs 〜より (simple comparison — A is more X than B). に比べて sets up the reference explicitly and tends to elaborate on the difference; より is a direct comparative marker within a single sentence.

name: 〜のみ
description:
のみ is a formal/literary equivalent of だけ, meaning "only", "just", or "alone". It restricts scope to the noun or clause it follows, excluding everything else. Unlike だけ, のみ is rarely used in casual conversation and is more common in written language, official announcements, and formal registers.

のみならず ("not only") and のみで ("only with") are common extensions. のみ can follow verbs (dictionary form + のみ) in formal writing.
usage: Noun + のみ / Verb (dictionary) + のみ

examples.japanese: 関係者のみ入場できます。
examples.romaji: Kankeisha nomi nyuujou dekimasu.
examples.english: Only authorized personnel may enter.

examples.japanese: 残るのみとなった問題を解決しなければならない。
examples.romaji: Nokoru nomi to natta mondai wo kaiketsu shinakereba naranai.
examples.english: We must resolve the only remaining issue.

notes: のみ (formal written) = だけ (casual spoken). In formal contexts such as signs, legal documents, and academic writing, のみ is strongly preferred over だけ. のみならず (not only ~, but also ~) is a common N2 expression derived from this.

name: 〜の結果
description:
結果 (けっか) means "result" or "as a result of ~". When used as a grammar pattern (〜の結果 or Verb + 結果), it expresses that the main clause is the outcome of the preceding action or process. It is used in both positive and negative outcomes.

The construction can follow a nominalized verb phrase (〜した結果), a noun (努力の結果 = as a result of effort), and often connects to a conclusion or final state.
usage: Verb (た-form) + 結果 / Noun + の結果

examples.japanese: 長い間話し合った結果、計画を変更することにした。
examples.romaji: Nagai aida hanashiatta kekka, keikaku wo henkou suru koto ni shita.
examples.english: As a result of a long discussion, we decided to change the plan.

examples.japanese: 努力の結果、試験に合格できた。
examples.romaji: Doryoku no kekka, shiken ni goukaku dekita.
examples.english: As a result of my efforts, I was able to pass the exam.

notes: 結果 vs 末に: both follow a process and lead to an outcome. 結果 is neutral and factual; 末に (sue ni, N1) implies a long or difficult process with an emotional weight to the conclusion. 話し合った末に (after much deliberation) is more dramatic than 話し合った結果 (as a result of the discussion).

name: 〜ばかり
description:
〜ばかり has several uses. (1) 'Just did' — right after a ta-form verb, indicates an action just completed: 食べたばかりだ (I just ate). (2) 'Nothing but,' 'only' — with a noun or plain verb, expresses exclusivity often with a negative connotation: 文句ばかり言う (does nothing but complain). (3) Approximately — follows numbers: 三つばかり (about three).

The nuance of 'nothing but' often carries criticism or dissatisfaction. ばかりいる after te-form means someone keeps doing something to excess: テレビを見てばかりいる (does nothing but watch TV all day).
usage: Noun + ばかり / Verb (ta) + ばかり

examples.japanese: 彼女はゲームばかりしている。
examples.romaji: Kanojo wa gēmu bakari shite iru.
examples.english: She does nothing but play games.

examples.japanese: 今起きたばかりなので、まだ眠いです。
examples.romaji: Ima okita bakari na no de, mada nemui desu.
examples.english: I just woke up, so I'm still sleepy.

notes: Three key ばかり patterns: (1) Verb-た + ばかり = just finished. (2) Noun/Verb + ばかり = nothing but / only (often negative connotation). (3) Number + ばかり = approximately. Don't confuse the 'just finished' meaning with the 'only' meaning — context and attachment form are key.

name: 〜はずがない
description:
〜はずがない expresses strong disbelief based on logical reasoning — 'there's no way that,' 'it cannot possibly be.' The speaker denies a possibility because it contradicts what they know: 彼が嘘をつくはずがない (there's no way he would lie — I know him well). The denial is based on expectations, not emotion.

はずがない attaches to plain forms. It differs from わけがない (which is more logically driven): はずがない is based on violated expectations and prior knowledge; わけがない is based on pure logical impossibility. In practice, the two are often interchangeable.

examples.japanese: そんなことが起こるはずがない。
examples.romaji: Sonna koto ga okoru hazu ga nai.
examples.english: There's no way such a thing could happen.

examples.japanese: 彼が嘘をつくはずがない。彼は正直な人だ。
examples.romaji: Kare ga uso wo tsuku hazu ga nai. Kare wa shōjiki na hito da.
examples.english: There's no way he would lie. He's an honest person.

notes: Both 〜はずがない and 〜わけがない translate as 'there's no way,' but はずがない focuses on violated expectations (based on what I know about the person/situation), while わけがない focuses on logical impossibility (there is no logical basis for it). The distinction is subtle and context-dependent.

name: 〜は言うまでもなく
description:
